text: Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries** **Section Twelve: Miscellaneous** **Records of Grand Talk** **Category: Novels (Strange Tales)** **Preface** We respectfully submit that *Records of Grand Talk* is a three-volume work by Tang Dynasty's Kang Bian. Wang Dingbao's *Miscellaneous Notes* records it as "Tang Bian," likely a transcription error. The *Tang Book's Catalogue of Arts and Literature* lists it as "Kang Peng," and the two characters align in meaning, though it is unclear which is correct. However, all references to it are as "Bian," suggesting that the *Tang Book* may have been in error. Kang Bian was from Chiyang, passed the imperial examination in the fourth year of Ganfu (877 AD), and held the position of *Shangshu Lang* in the Imperial Library. This work was completed in the second year of Ganning (895 AD), and it records events from the Tianbao era (742-756 AD) and beyond, occasionally interspersing commentary. It consists of forty entries. When cross-referenced with the *Taiping Guangji*, they correspond perfectly, indicating that either the entire work was included at the time or it was later compiled from the *Taiping Guangji*. This edition, printed by Chen Dao Ren's bookstore in Lin'an Prefecture, is likely a copy of the Song dynasty manuscript. For example, in the entry about General Pan, the note suggests that the name might be "Pan Gu Lu," but in the current version of *Swordsmen's Tales*, it is incorrectly written as "Pan Xi Su," making it difficult to understand. This shows that this edition is superior. Among its contents, there is an entry about Yuan Zhen, who, in his old age, passed the examination and paid homage to Li He. The *Ancient Tales from the Yu Hut Pavilion* notes: "Yuan Zhen's passing the examination was not in his old age, and he was not Li He's senior. How could he, in his old age, go to Li He's door and be considered frivolous? Such criticism is unfounded. The tales of the *Bian Office* are half based on hearsay, and their authenticity and falsehood are mixed. This has been the case since ancient times, and they cannot be entirely taken as true or false. Readers must examine their merits and faults. This does not mean that the entire work should be discarded.

Chief Editor Officials:** - Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Ji Yun - Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Lu Xi Xiong - Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Sun Shi Yi **Chief Proofreader Official:** - [Official] Lu Fei Chi **Imperial Decree Collection, Volume:** - Subcategory Twelve: Novel Category Two [Strange Occurrences] **Book I:** Emperor Xuanzong's Night Summoning of Hanlin Scholars** **Liu Ping's Encounter with Evil Spirits of An Lushan** **Wang Wei Saves a Singer or Dancer Who Was a High-Ranking Official** **The Rapids of the Censor** **The Cloud Ladder of Generals Hun and Li Xi Ping Against Zhu Ci** **General Pan Loses a Pearl** **Li Yehou Rescues Duan Tingzhi** **Continued: The Horse of Xukun** **Dragon Attendant Advises with a Ceremonial Tablet** **Meng Cairen Skilled in Singing** **Yuan Xiang Exchanges Snow for a Golden County Magistrate** **Guo Xun Sees Poor Ghosts** **Pei Jingong Meets an Elder at Tianjin Bridge** **Di Weiqian Asks for Rain** **Wang Shizhong Writes a Poem** **A Daoist Advises Xiahou Qiaogong** **The Dragon Moves the Spring of Mount Hua** **Tian Penglang Steals a Jade Pillow** **The Great Flood in Luoyang** **Li Zhuyan Knows White Lingong **Book II:** **The House of Prime Minister Liu** **The House of Prime Minister Li** **Peony at Cien Temple** **Guan Wandi Meets a Strong Man** **Zhang Jihong Encounters a Bad New Wife

The True Person of the Jade Rhododendron Hall descended. The Chancellor made offerings. Cui Daochu ate well water fish. The strong men of Luoyang. The Fengxiang Prefecture raised an army to fight the rebels. The painting at the Laojun Temple. Bai Fu rode a boat. Yan Shijun met a recluse in Mount Zhongnan. Wei Zhuang's hawk cried. On the day of destiny, hail fell. Li Sheng saw a divine being leave wine. Talking about the Daoist. Expanding the poem of the exiled immortal's lament. The Hall of Containing the Beginning. Qujiang. The True Body. Yuan Xiangguo visited Li He. 钦定四库全书 剧谈录卷上 唐 康骈 撰宣宗夜召翰林学士 宣宗皇帝圣政钦明光宅天下常欲刑清俗富有宵衣旰食之懐仄席竚贤每如不及令狐相国自吴兴郡守授司郎中未居内署初与学士对便以为有宰辅之才一夕于禁林寓直忽有中使来召行百余歩至于便殿遣内人秉烛之引于御榻之前上自宣令坐问卿来从江表见彼中甿庶安否廉察郡守字人求瘼之道如何朕常思四海之大九州之广虽明君不能自理常须良弼贤佐迩来窃窥朝廷皆未覩其忠赤相国降堦俯伏曰圣意如此微臣便合得罪上曰卿才为翰林学士所职者朕之丝纶向来之言本不相 The Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, with his wise and enlightened governance, desired to bring peace and prosperity to the land. He often worked tirelessly, rising early and retiring late, always seeking the best in his administration. He was eager to find and appoint virtuous officials, and felt as though he could never find enough. When the Chancellor Linghu Chong was appointed from his position as the Prefect of Wujiang to the post of Langzhong in the Imperial Secretariat, he had not yet taken up his duties in the inner office. One night, while lodging in the Imperial Library, he was suddenly summoned by an imperial messenger. After walking more than a hundred steps, he arrived at a side hall where a eunuch was holding a candle to wait for him. The messenger led him to the imperial bedchamber, where the Emperor personally ordered him to sit and asked, "Have you come from Jiangnan? How are the common people there? What is the situation with the Prefects and the people in your region? I often think about the vastness of the empire and the multitude of its people. Even a wise ruler cannot manage everything alone; he must rely on good ministers and capable assistants. Recently, I have observed that the court has not seen enough of their loyalty and dedication. Chancellor, you have descended the steps and bowed, saying, 'If the Emperor thinks so, I am at fault.' The Emperor replied, 'Your talents as a翰林学士 are to serve as my words and deeds. The previous statements were not meant to be taken literally.'

After some time, the emperor again commanded the chancellor to sit and ordered him to serve wine in a jade cup. A small table was placed on the imperial bed, and there were two books on the table. Pointing to the books, the emperor said to the chancellor, "During my leisure time when I am not attending to government affairs, I never fail to read historical records. This reader is a scroll written by the previous dynasty about the Golden Mirror, which is the *Shangshu: Dayu Mu*. Then I asked whether you had read the Golden Mirror. You replied that the books written by Emperor Wen contain the essentials of governing the country and managing oneself, and you read and recited them without ceasing." The emperor said, "You try to summarize the main points." The chancellor knelt before the emperor and loudly recited, "When it comes to chaos, it has never failed to rely on unworthy people; when it comes to order, it has never failed to rely on loyal and virtuous people. Relying on loyal and virtuous people brings the happiness of the entire world; relying on unworthy people brings the calamity of the entire world." The emperor stopped him and said, "Every time I read to this point, I never fail to reread it three times before moving on." The book also says, "Appoint virtuous people without hesitation and remove evil people without doubt. This is the principle that one should use these words to achieve peace and prosperity." The chancellor danced with joy and said, "My father often said that the Golden Mirror is a timeless maxim."

Without intelligence and literary insight, one cannot explore its profound mysteries. How much more so the ways of Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, which are found in the classics, edicts, and admonitions. Your Majesty does not consider the yellow carriage as a sign of respect; you often observe it day and night. Intending to select the virtuous and promote the good, to make all undertakings flourish. Thus, the achievements would surpass those of all previous kings and even the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors. The emperor said, "Previously, I admired your talents and abilities. Today, I have seen your literary skills. Standing by the hall for a long time, I told the eunuch to say, 'Hold a candle and escort the scholar to his residence.' When returning to the imperial garden, the night was halfway through. All considered the favor shown to the close ministers unparalleled. From this point on, the attention became even deeper. After living for over a year, he became the chancellor. From the position of prefect to the highest office, it took only two years. Once, after returning from the imperial altar, the magistrate of Weinan, Zhao Gu, presented a poem. 'The osprey is among the noble clouds, the ice is in the pot. To represent the talents and industries of heaven, serving the grand plans. To share the glory of Yi Zhi and Fu Zhu, who were given red doors as a sign of honor. To be as distinguished as Wang Shang, entering the painting. Last night, the stars returned to the sword and shoes. Last year, the wind and moon filled the rivers and lakes. Not knowing if there is much leisure time for state affairs, still allowing the family to recite and harmonize the poems.'"

The discourse says that those who possess great talent and resources sometimes achieve success, but if they seek favor through flattery or compromise, they may not be recognized. If a wise ruler intended to appoint virtuous individuals but his ministers lacked the ability to speak on their own, this would only invite criticism for false advancement and the theory of illness retreat and shame. Such extraordinary favor and kindness would not reach them. Therefore, a gentleman sets his mind on lofty goals and disciplines himself, and encountering good fortune is indeed well guarded by fate. Liu Ping saw the spirits accompanying An Lushan. During the Xiantong era, there was a Five Classics Doctor named Lu Jia who practiced Daoist longevity techniques and claimed to have been born during the Sui Dynasty. Distinguished officials of the court with long-standing virtue all said they had seen him since his youth. The entire world believed and venerated him, and he was said to have an exceptionally long life. During the An Lushan Rebellion, he went into hiding in the Zhongnan Mountains. Later, he sometimes appeared and sometimes disappeared. Previously, Chancellor Linghu had told him about the stories of Laozi and Zhuangzi, and he gradually began serving in the capital. He often talked with the Daoist practitioner Liu Ping, his close friend who practiced Daoism. During the Tianbao period, Liu Ping lived in the regions of Qi and Lu. He was particularly skilled in breathing exercises and could see things at night without the aid of lamps or candles. When An Lushan was in Fan Yang, he offered generous gifts to attract him to his service. Liu Ping saw that An Lushan was often accompanied by dozens of ghostly beings with strange and bizarre forms, holding incense burners and umbrellas as a retinue. He found it strange and unusual, and thought that An Lushan must be a person of great talent or virtue. When An Lushan went to court, he and Liu Ping traveled together by carriage. They reached Huayin County, where Ye the Celestial Master performed the ritual of offering dragons at the Western Mountain. Liu Ping saw two young boys in green robes ride on empty space, and the spirits guarding An Lushan all abandoned their incense burners and umbrellas, running away in disarray. Liu Ping then realized that An Lushan was being assisted by evil spirits and would not achieve a successful conclusion through proper means. When An Lushan returned to Fan Yang, Liu Ping fled into Mount Hua and went into hiding.

Wang Wei Revives a Singer for Cui Gong Wang Wei, the young man from Fengxiang, who was the nephew of Wang Ning, a high-ranking official, was around fifteen or sixteen years old. One day, he played with children in an orchard and bamboo grove when he saw two dry heads buried in manure. He ordered a servant to choose a clean place to bury the heads and offer wine as a sacrifice. After several dark and gloomy nights, he suddenly heard a sound outside the window. After a long time, he asked, "We have received great kindness from the young master, and we are no longer in the wilderness. We do not know how to repay you." They offered to inform him of any good or bad omens in the future. For several years, Wang Wei established a connection with the spirits. Cui Gong, the Chancellor and a high-ranking official responsible for finance and taxation, was familiar with Wang Wei. One evening, Cui Gong invited Wang Wei to drink at home. They enjoyed a family brew, and as they became more cheerful, Cui Gong mentioned that he had a young woman skilled in singing, obtained from friends and relatives. He then ordered his attendants to summon her. After a long time, she did not arrive. Cui Gong suddenly returned from inside, having just finished dressing. She suddenly fell ill with heart pain and asked for some soup. Cui Gong was surprised and asked what happened. She said she had just seen a man in a short-sleeved red robe riding away on a horse. Before he could finish speaking, a servant rushed in to report that she had suddenly fallen ill and could not be saved. Cui Gong was deeply saddened. Wang Wei secretly said that there might be a way to save her, but it would require a white bull's head and a measure of wine. He then ordered his attendants to try to find them at the office of the Chancellor.

A messenger was sent to the eastern market's meat shop to purchase a cow's head at a good price. Upon bringing the cow's head back, the messenger was instructed to have the singer, who was being supported by a staff, placed in a clean room. A table was set up with wine in a clay bowl, and a horizontal board was used to support the cow's head. The room was sealed, and the messenger was warned to keep watch. It was said that when the dawn drum beats and a cow's roar is heard, the door should be opened quickly to save the singer. After the messenger left, there was no sound for a long time. Suddenly, the drum sounded, and indeed, a cow's roar was heard. The door was opened, and it was found that the singer was barely breathing, and the wine in the bowl had completely dried up. The singer's eyes were filled with rage, and they seemed to be looking outside. Several days later, the singer recounted the previous events: that evening, after finishing her preparations, she was urgently summoned and went out on horseback. After traveling several miles, she saw a beautifully decorated building. Inside, a feast was set up, and music was playing. The guests were all young noblemen dressed in purple and red. When the singer arrived, they were very happy and treated her as a courtesan. They were enjoying themselves when suddenly a loud cry was heard, shaking the courtyard. Everyone lost their composure and looked at each other. The music and entertainment stopped. Soon, a figure with the head of a cow, over ten feet tall, holding a spear, rushed in. Everyone fled in panic, but the singer remained. The figure with the cow's head took her to the front of the steps, carried her on his back, and left. After walking a few dozen steps, the singer suddenly realized she was lying in the room. Later, when the high official asked her about the events, the singer finally revealed everything.

**The Censor's Sandbar** In Yique County of Henan Prefecture, which borders a large stream, whenever an assistant was appointed to the Censorate, a small sandbar would first appear in the water, with clear and beautiful gravel and gold sand. The County Magistrate, Cattle, upon learning of this, suddenly heard that a sandbar had emerged. The next day, he and his colleagues set up a banquet on the pavilion to observe it. He then summoned the elderly officials to inquire about the matter. An old clerk said, "This must be the sign of a Censor from the Censorate, not from the Western Censorate. If it is from the Western Censorate, there should be a pair of pheasants standing before and after the sandbar, which the locals use as a rule." The County Magistrate secretly pondered that none of the county officials could surpass him. He raised his cup and prayed, "If it is possible to have a sandbar, why not a pair of pheasants?" Before the banquet ended, a pair of pheasants suddenly flew down. Within ten days, he was appointed as a Censor of the Censorate. **The Cloud Ladder of Li Xiping** During the rebellion led by Zhu Ci, Emperor Dezong's carriage departed to the imperial retreat at Fanyang. At that time, the frontier military governors had already raised armies to escort the emperor. Zhu Ci personally led his troops to the city walls. A Buddhist monk from the Western Bright Temple, who had fallen into the hands of the rebels, was very ingenious and taught Zhu Ci to build a cloud ladder, which was over ninety feet tall. On top of it, there were wooden houses and watchtowers that could overlook the city. Li Xiping, the County Magistrate, reported to Emperor Dezong, saying, "The enemy's attack is fierce, and their cloud ladder is also formidable. If we let them advance, we are truly afraid that we cannot withstand them. While they are still far away, please allow us to use sharp troops to crush them." He then led five thousand imperial troops out to meet the enemy. At that time, they had tied straw bundles behind their formation, agreeing to ignite them when the battle was intense. However, the wind was unfavorable, and the fire could not be lit. The two generals poured wine, spoke solemnly, and bowed while praying, invoking the assistance of heaven. The divine intervention helped the righteous cause, and the sage emperor moved the gods.

The Rebel Zhu Ci, harboring treacherous intentions and secretly wielding weapons of violence, dared to launch a reckless attack upon the imperial carriage. Now, with a gathered army, he threatens the emperor and presses upon the city walls. Generals Jie and others vowed to devote their loyalty and resolve to destroy the evil forces, hoping to restore the state and the emperor’s divine authority, which had not yet faded. They would see the enemy’s faction melt away like ice, using siege towers to bring about their downfall. Thus, their words and feelings were resolute and impassioned, and their soldiers were a hundredfold more courageous. Soon, the tide of battle turned suddenly; the troops advanced with drums and shouting, fire raged and winds howled, smoke and dust filled the sky, siege towers burned, and the enemy soldiers fled in defeat. Emperor Dezong, standing on a tower to observe, was acclaimed with cries of “Long Live the Emperor!” by all within and beyond the city. When the capital was finally recaptured, the two generals’ achievements stood at the forefront, and they were honored with fiefdoms, their names inscribed upon bells and tripods. They had consistently supported the state from beginning to end. Later, Li Shitou, the military governor, had four sons, each given command of a region. Their loyalty and valor remain prominent to this day. Li Shitou once served in the Left Guards for a long time without receiving a promotion. He heard that Sang Daomao was skilled in physiognomy and carried a piece of silk to visit him early in the morning. At the time, many people sought Sang’s counsel, but most were not received. When Li Shitou arrived, Sang personally welcomed him, prepared food and wine, and showed great sincerity. Afterward, he asked Li, saying, “One day, when you have built great merit and power, you will be unparalleled in status. If you ever hold authority in your hands, you may entrust your life to me.” Li could not understand the reason behind this, and only felt ashamed and replied with a simple “Yes.” Sang then asked him to exchange the silk he had brought for one of Li’s undergarments, and requested that he write his name on it, saying, “One day, if you see this, you will remember my prediction regarding Zhu Ci’s rebellion.” At the time, Sang was in the rebel court. After the capital was recaptured, those who had followed the rebellion were all executed. Li Shitou was ordered to carry out the sentence. As Sang was about to be executed, he pleaded for clemency, offering the undergarment as proof. Li Shitou petitioned the emperor, stating that Sang was innocent, and was thus ordered to be pardoned.

A General Loses His Prayer Beads A wealthy and influential man from the capital, General Pan, lived in Guangde Ward [his name is unknown, but he is called Pan Gu Lu by the people]. Originally from the region of Xiang and Han, he often traveled by boat to make a profit and once docked at the riverbank. A monk came to beg for food and stayed for several days, during which Pan offered generous alms. The monk said to Pan, "Your appearance, character, and demeanor are different from ordinary merchants. And from your wife and children onwards, you will all enjoy great fortune." He then gave Pan a string of jade prayer beads, saying that they would not only bring wealth but also future official rank. After several years of trading, Pan accumulated vast wealth and became as wealthy as Tao Zhu, later holding a high position in the capital. He always kept the prayer beads in a brocade bag and a jade box, placing them in the main hall. Every month on the first day, he would take them out and worship them. One day, when he opened the box, the beads were missing. However, the seals were intact, and nothing else was missing. Thus, he was terrified and believed this was a sign that his family would be destroyed. A person in charge of the household, who knew a man named Wang Chao from the Imperial Censorate, who was over eighty years old, told him about the incident. Wang Chao said it was strange.

Alas, this is not a thief who steals openly. Let us try to find out whether it can be obtained. On another day, Zhao happened to pass by the northern street of Shengye Lane, at a time when the spring rain had just cleared. There was a girl with three buns on her head, about seventeen or eighteen years old, dressed in tattered clothes and wearing wooden clogs, standing by the side of the road under a locust tree. A young soldier happened to kick a ball and hit her, reaching several zhang high. Then the onlookers gradually gathered, but Zhao alone found this strange. Afterwards, he followed her, stopping at the short lane near the northern gate of Shengye Lane, where he lived with his mother, as she made a living by sewing. Zhao, being curious, got to know her through other matters, and they became like uncle and nephew, but their living conditions were very poor. They slept together on an earthen bed, and the fire did not burn for several days. When they prepared dishes, there were often rare delicacies from both land and water. The Dongting oranges from Wu were first presented to the chancellor as a favor, and they were not available in the capital. She secretly gave one to Zhao, saying, "There is a person who came out from inside and had a decisive character." Zhao was very suspicious of him. They met like this for a whole year. One day, Zhao invited him to a feast and spoke to him calmly and slowly.

The uncle had a profound truth he wished to tell his nephew, but did not know how to go about it. The girl said, "I always feel deeply grateful for the great favor, yet regret not being able to repay it. If there is an opportunity to exert effort, I will surely go through fire and water." Chao said, "General Pan has lost his precious prayer beads; do you know if he is aware of this?" The girl smiled and asked, "How do you know?" Chao thought she did not keep it very secret. She then said, "Nephew, you can search for and prepare generous gifts to repay him." The girl said, "Do not tell others. I occasionally played with friends and eventually returned the item, but due to procrastination or lack of time, it was not returned immediately." The uncle said, "The next day, early in the morning, meet me at the pagoda courtyard of the Ci'en Temple. I know that someone has left pearls here." Chao went as scheduled. At that time, the temple gate was just opening, and the pagoda door was still locked. The woman was already there and told Chao, "A short while later, look up at the tower, and you will see something." After finishing speaking, she left. She moved as fast as a bird. Suddenly, on the wheel of the stupa, she raised her hand to show Chao. The pearls suddenly came down, and she said to Chao, "Can take them back, do not worry about the value of the pearls." Chao went directly to General Pan and fully explained the matter. He secretly gave gold, jade, silk, and brocade as a gift. The next day, when he visited, the room was already empty.

Cao Xue, the Grand Secretariat, often heard that there were many chivalrous people in the capital. When he became a county magistrate, he secretly inquired of those around him and brought up and fully recounted previous events. He then inquired about what General Pan had said, which matched what Cao Xue had recounted. Li Yehou, a historical figure, was known for saving Dou Tingzhi. During the Baoying era, the junior official Dou Tingzhi was stationed in Luoyang and often respectfully attended to the fortune-teller Hu Lusheng. Hu Lusheng's predictions of good and bad omens were always accurate, and he visited very frequently. Both young and old had no doubts about him. One day, in the early morning, Hu Lusheng entered the door and was quite sorrowful and regretful. Dou Tingzhi asked him for a long time, and then he said, "Your family will soon face a great disaster, and the entire family will likely be wiped out. It is not expected to happen immediately, but it is not far off." The whole family wept and asked for a way to survive. Hu Lusheng said, "Only by meeting Huangzhong Jun and Zhigu Zi can one be saved. However, Huangzhong Jun is difficult to meet casually. But if one meets Zhigu Zi, there will be no danger." He described his appearance and attire in detail and agreed to search for him within ten days. Thus, Dou Tingzhi and his brothers, relatives, wife, children, and servants searched for Zhigu Zi day and night in Luoyang. At that time, Li Yehou was in mourning for a close relative and lived in Heqing County.

Because of visiting relatives and friends, he rode a slow donkey into Luoyang and arrived at the south side of Zhong Bridge, where he encountered a high official who yielded the way. The donkey he was riding suddenly became frightened and ran away, directly entering the residence of Dou Tingzhi. With the servant together, they visited his door just as Dou Tingzhi's chariots and horses were arranged to depart. Suddenly seeing the Duke of Ye, all were startled and stepped back. Soon, someone came out and said, "This is the residence of the External Official Dou, and the lost donkey is in the stable." They invited the guests to sit. The External Official had wished to pay respects several times like this, but the Duke of Ye could not help but attend the gathering. Dou Tingzhi then stepped down from the steps and bowed, received with great hospitality, and stayed for two nights. Even the wife, children, and grandchildren were treated with the same family courtesy. After several days, he bid farewell, giving gifts of great value, but said, "On the day of your promotion, he wishes to entrust his entire family to you." The Duke of Ye stayed at Heqing for two nights, traveling back and forth on the road. When Zhu Ci rebelled, Dou Tingzhi was just inspecting Shaanxi. The emperor's carriage went out to Fengtian, and Dou Tingzhi was captured by the rebels. When the imperial carriage returned to the capital, Emperor Dezong first ordered his execution. The Duke of Ye was summoned from the Southern Mountain to the imperial presence and became Chancellor. Because of listing the crimes of officials, he then requested that Dou Tingzhi's sentence be commuted to death. The emperor did not understand and asked, "Do you think he is a close relative of Prince Ning?" [Dou Tingzhi's sister was the wife of Prince Ning] Even if this is not the case, is there no other reason to allow him to live? Just tell me." Then he detailed the previous events to the emperor, and thus, he was specially pardoned. The Duke of Ye then reported to the emperor, who secretly sent an eunuch by night on a mission to Shaanxi to inquire. Dou reported the matter, and Emperor Dezong said, "The previous statement about Yellow Middle Lord refers to me, I do not know why you call you Green Valley Son." [Some say that the former residence of Li Xiang was in Lingcheng, which is before Qing Valley and after Zhuo Valley, perhaps this is what is meant.]

During the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (Suantong) and Emperor Yizong (Ganfu), there was a physician in the capital named Xun Kun, who held the title of Director of Waterways. He had acquired the skills of Qin He, a renowned physician, and could diagnose by pulse to predict auspicious or inauspicious outcomes, even the gains and losses, and the times and dates, could be foretold. One day, a high-ranking official from Yanzhong, who was about to present matters to the court, suddenly fell ill with a severe wind disease. Many doctors treated him without success. The official was carried to Kun, who prescribed a few doses of medicine, and the official recovered. The official rewarded Kun with a large amount of gold and silk, and also gave him a horse as a gift. The horse had a very unique skeletal structure but often stumbled and tripped, despite being controlled with a bit and bridle and spurred with a whip, its steady and slow nature could not be changed. Kun, considering the horse wasted feed and grain, entrusted someone to sell it at a low price. Those seeking a good horse would try it and find it just as it was before, and it remained unsold for months. A man named Wang, who lived nearby and traded at the door of a powerful official, was quite poor. He came to see Kun and said, "There is a military officer from Qingzhou about to depart and needs a horse and saddle for his journey. He also knows that the horse is not suitable for racing but wants it to be used for pulling and controlling." Kun simply gave it away as a useless animal and did not set a price for selling the horse. Wang took the horse and was satisfied.

For ten days, Wang Sheng did not appear, and so it was thought he had abandoned or lost the horse. But one day he returned and paid ten thousand in cash. Kun, having obtained a good price, gave him ten thousand as a gift. Soon after, Wang Sheng changed his clothes, arranged for servants and horses, and even his attendants, wife, and children were dressed in fine and fresh garments. Someone said that Wang Sheng had sold the horse for silk, gold, and cloth worth several million. Kun was greatly surprised and inquired about the matter. Wang Sheng at first had no ready explanation. Kun said, "I obtained a good price for a useless animal, but I am surprised that such a poor and inferior horse could be worth so much." Wang Sheng then said, "At first I brought the horse to the military commissioner at Qing She, and when he lifted his foot, it seemed to be restrained." When he was returning, on the way he met an official from the small horse pavilion, who sent him to stay for a night’s trial, and then the horse was not returned. Kun secretly inquired of his attendants and learned that several days ago Wei Bo had presented a horse whose size and appearance were exactly the same. The emperor regularly rode a fine polo horse, and there was no match for it. On the day it was to arrive, they sent people to train it, and it moved as swiftly as the wind. Now, having been presented to the court for several days, the gifts given to its owner were very generous. Wang Sheng then demanded the price and paid forty million in return. Thus, the value of an object depends on the time and circumstances. If it does not meet its rightful owner, even the inferior and superior horses cannot be distinguished. Thus, one understands that the plow in the field of Nuo and the construction at Fu Yan, which did not meet the right path, are not different from this.

During the Kai Cheng era, there was a fortune-teller named Long Fuben who was blind but skilled in hearing sounds and divining bones. He made predictions about auspicious and inauspicious signs without fail. Whenever he handled symbols of official rank, such as ivory tablets and bamboo tablets, he could predict one's official position, salary, and lifespan. Cui Qi was renowned in the world for his literary achievements, and scholars and officials were all eager and attentive. They counted on their fingers and toes, hoping for high rank and success. At that time, a high-ranking official named Xiao, also in the office of censors, visited him on the same day and presented him with the bamboo tablet he was holding. Dragon Fuben held the high-ranking official Xiao's tablet for a long time and placed it on the table, saying it was a chancellor's tablet. Next, he took Cui Qi's tablet and said it was a high-ranking official's tablet. Cui Qi heard this and was not happy. The chancellor said, "Empty words are not worth worrying about." After several months, when they were waiting to see the chancellor, Li Deyu, who was wielding power and awe-inspiring in the court and the countryside, suddenly left. Cui Qi covered his face with his hand, still laughing, and Li Deyu looked at him. Those who heard about it were all filled with fear and trembling. Within ten days, he was appointed as the magistrate of Heqing County and died shortly after. Later, the high-ranking official Xiao ascended the clear path, from the position of governor of Zhe West to the head of the Ministry of Revenue, and then became a high-ranking official, all as predicted by Dragon Fuben.

Since the reigns of Emperor Xianzong and Emperor Wenzong, there have been many fortune-tellers in the capital, but very few of their predictions have been accurate. What I have heard is that the scholar Ding Zhong is skilled in predicting auspicious and inauspicious events, and he has frequently had remarkable accuracy. At the time, a high-ranking official in charge of salt and iron administration frequently had news of becoming a prime minister. The high-ranking official Lu held significant power and was not in harmony with him. One day, Ding Zhong was at his private residence in Xinchang, and the official happened to visit. Lu said, "I am a friend of his. Could you please observe him carefully through the curtain and tell me if he will eventually become a chancellor?" Ding Zhong prepared a feast and stayed for several hours. After he left, Lu asked, "What did you see?" Ding Zhong replied, "He will certainly become a chancellor, and it will happen within a few months." Lu laughed and said, "He is a close relative of the emperor and will become a Salt and Iron Commissioner." Ding Zhong disagreed and asked, "How does he compare to Emperor Xuanzong's favor?"

The Chancellor of the Court, Viceroy of Zheng, said, "How can it be compared?" The Viceroy replied, "Zheng was highly regarded by Emperor Xuan for a long time, yet ultimately did not become a chancellor. How could it be that human affairs can be measured? I am well acquainted with the Censorate; today I saw him. Observing his bone structure, he is truly of noble character. Next, his bearing is elegant and upright, his manners courteous and respectful. He is like a large vessel of a hundred measures, still half empty. How can it be that he does not benefit from rank and position? If he does not hold office for more than a month, I dare not come to your door." The recluse scholar said, "You can be called broad and far-reaching." Afterwards, within ten days, he indeed ascended to high office. The Viceroy of the Road always highly praised officials when he saw them, and from this, his fame echoed through the capital city. Many carriages and horses came to his door, and whatever he said, his words all came true. Later, he lived on Zhongnan Mountain, and people interested in him also came to his place. The Shangcai Woman was good at singing. She was favored among the concubines of Emperor Wu of the Tang Dynasty, and no one could compare to her. One day, when the emperor's health was not well, he called her and asked, "If my illness is serious, where will you go?" She replied, "With my humble body, enjoying the emperor's favor, after the emperor's death, I will not live again." That day, she was ordered to sing the song "He Manzi" in front of the emperor's bed. Her tone was sad and poignant, and all who heard her shed tears. When the emperor died, she mourned for several days and died in the palace. Close attendants buried her with a small coffin beside the palace. During the burial, the emperor's coffin was too heavy to be lifted. Those who knew said, "Is it not the concubine?" Then, they carried her coffin to accompany the emperor and buried her beside the emperor's tomb. That year, literary scholars wrote poems or titles, considering her none could surpass the historical figures Feng Yuan and Ban Ji. Those who knew, Zhang Hu, wrote a poem saying, "Occasionally, due to clear singing and frequent performances in the palace for twenty springs, she sang 'He Manzi' once, and in the netherworld, one must mourn the Shangcai Woman."

Commander Yuan Xue, the Magistrate of Jinyang County During the time when Li Qian, a high-ranking official, was stationed in Fengxiang, a subordinate county magistrate and his people were plowing the fields and discovered a jar of horse-shoe-shaped gold. [Note: According to the *Book of Han*, Emperor Wu of Han had previously issued an edict stating that the appearance of gold in the east and the sighting of a white unicorn and divine horse were auspicious signs. It was suggested that gold should be cast into the shape of a unicorn to match these omens, and later, the common people followed this practice.] The villagers delivered the gold to the county office, and the report was about to be submitted to the governor's office. The county magistrate was delighted to have found a treasure and wished to claim this as a special achievement. Worried that the public treasury was not strictly guarded, he placed it in his private room. After a few days, he opened it again with the officials, but it was all turned into clay blocks. When the gold from the jar was unearthed, all the villagers came to inspect it, and it was suddenly changed. No one was not shocked, and he reported the situation to the governor. The advisors all said it was a trick to swap it. They then sent a judge and several military officers to investigate the matter. The village that found the gold all testified against him. The county magistrate was overwhelmed and humiliated by the crowd and could not clear his name. And his humiliation grew even worse. He was thus convicted of swapping the gold, and the evidence was all present, but the investigation was not yet complete.

The official used his power to order the detention and threatening of a servant or subordinate with legal punishment. Some said he was hidden in filth, others said he was thrown into water. The situation was chaotic and unjust, and a case was formulated and presented to higher authorities. The duke viewed the case and became even angrier. Soon after, there was a banquet where the guests stopped drinking and talked about the matter. All the seated guests expressed amazement, some saying it was like the man from Qi trying to steal a cook's pot, others saying it was like Yang Zhen's integrity. They laughed and chatted for a while, thinking that theft was not worth being surprised about. At the time, Chancellor Yuan Zi was also in the hall, bowing his head with no words. The official looked at him several times and said, "Is the mayor not a close relative or ally of the judge?" Chancellor Yuan said, "I have no prior relationship with him." The official said, "Why are you not happy about his crime?" Chancellor Yuan said, "I suspect this matter is not yet clear; please ask the chancellor to investigate further." The duke said, "The situation of the gold exchange is very clear. There must be another perspective; only the judge can uncover the truth." Chancellor Yuan said, "Yes," and ordered the case to be moved to the government office for investigation. He then ordered the examination of a jar, where thirty-five pieces were found. When questioned about the first piece obtained, it was confirmed to be the original.

He then searched among the shops for gold to cast into ingots of the same shape as the blocks. Once completed, he weighed half of it, which already amounted to three hundred pounds. Calculating the total amount of gold, it was not something two people could carry on bamboo poles. It became clear that by the time they started on the road, the gold had already turned to dust. The public's anxiety was greatly relieved, and the county magistrate was thus cleared of suspicion. The Marquis of Qian was deeply impressed and could not stop praising. He often said that his intelligence and ability were inferior to those who followed him on the path of officialdom. By the time of Emperor Dezong, they had all become chancellors. I have heard that gold hidden in the earth, when discovered, sometimes changes its form. In Dongdu's Dunhua Ward, there was a trace of a qilin (a mythical creature) seen in the dilapidated halls of Xingqing Temple. During the Xiantong period, Duke Bi ordered the reconstruction, and among the foundations, large jars containing white gold were found. The financial officers and thirty-three craftsmen were all afraid that the gold would be taken by the authorities. They then used wooden ladders to cover it and waited for it to become dark. At night, each wrapped the gold in clothing and took it home. The next morning, when opened, it was like hard soil cut into silver ingots. What is said here is exactly the same.

**De Xun and the Ghosts** De Xun, an interpreter, was once the magistrate of Linyang County but had not been promoted for a long time. Living in poverty in the capital, he was deeply impoverished and struggling. In the space between the living and the dead, there were two creatures that resembled apes or gibbons, wearing green and blue robes. They accompanied him in all his daily activities, both sleeping and waking. Whenever he intended to take action or seek something, they insisted on going with him, and the places they went to were obstructed by thorns, symbolizing difficulties or obstacles. Failing to obtain an official position or salary, he suffered from cold and hunger. Friends and relatives all regarded him with suspicion or hostility, trying to repel them using talismans or magic, or seeking refuge in the mountains and forests. For several years, nothing could be done to get rid of them. One night, he was in a clean room when the creatures suddenly came to say goodbye. They said, "We have been with you for a long time due to your misfortune. Now we will wait until the morning bell rings and leave. We will not return." De Xun was happy that they were leaving and asked where they were going. They said, "There are many like you in the world, but people do not see them. We are headed to the Wang family in Shengye坊, who have amassed a large fortune and will soon distribute it." De Xun asked again, "Why will their wealth be dispersed?" They replied, "We have already made plans with An Pinzi, leading to the dispersal of the wealth. The rest will melt away like ice and dissipate like fog. The morning bell suddenly rang, and they disappeared from sight." De Xun then rose, washed his mouth and face, and felt a sense of relief or change.

On that day, when he visited relatives and friends, everyone was impressed. They interacted for less than ten days. At the political hall, he made a statement and was appointed as a translator. Xun had a cousin named Zhang Sheng, who was an assistant to the Gold Guards and enjoyed the company of bold and heroic young men who enjoyed speed and novelty. When Zhang Sheng heard about this, he did not believe it. Xun knew of a wealthy family named Wang in the Shengye Fang district of Chang'an and went there. He stopped his horse and lingered, showing joy on his face. He then called his companions and ordered wine, starting a banquet, and they had a very enjoyable time. Zhang Sheng was among them and secretly inquired from those around, learning that the woman was good at singing. That day, she sang several songs, and Wang gave all the gold and silver as gifts. Everyone was surprised by her generosity. From then on, carriages and wealth were daily delivered to her door. Every time they gathered and drank, she did not hesitate to give away her wealth. Gradually, the business of making a living became someone else's, and within a few years, she became poor and scarce.

Péi Jigen, the Duke of Huai West, Encounters Old Men on Tianjin Bridge When Péi Jigen was in his humble beginnings, he stayed as a guest in Luoyang and often rode a slow donkey into the imperial city, just crossing the Tianjin Bridge. At that time, the Huai West region had been in turmoil for more than forty years, with prolonged warfare and conscription causing great hardship. It was unknown when it would be pacified. Suddenly, two elderly men standing by the bridge column spoke, saying, "We are worried that Cai Prefecture has not been pacified; we must wait for this person to become a general." Upon seeing Péi Jigen, they were surprised and retreated. A servant carrying a book bag followed behind, slightly apart. He heard the old men say, "We are just worried that Cai Prefecture has not been pacified; we must wait for this person." After returning, the servant reported the entire incident. Péi Jigen said, "You see me old and frail, just playing a joke." That autumn, he was recommended by the Eastern Prefecture and passed the imperial examination the following year. When he held the balance of power, the court discussed granting the military command to Wu Yuanji. Later, in the Yanqing Hall, the Emperor asked the prime minister. Péi Jigen replied, "Rebellious officials have been guarding for more than forty years. The holy court has been trying to accommodate, fearing that any action would harm the region. No one has heard of their submission, and they sit in control of one area. Granting them the military command would allow them to act wickedly. Your Majesty is wise and valiant, and the regional governors all wish to serve the imperial court. I request that a single edict be issued to mobilize troops and pacify the rebels." Thus, Péi Jigen was appointed as the military governor of Huai West and mobilized troops to attack. At the time, the generals of Chen, Xu, Bian, and Huai were stationed in Yancheng County. Péi Jigen led 50,000 elite troops to defend them, receiving orders and advancing with drums beating, directly reaching the walls of Cai Prefecture. After only two months, the rebels were captured and presented, and Huai West was finally pacified. Later, he entered the court and served in the central government, receiving six promotions to the position of Grand Tutor and serving as Chief Secretary and Director of the Secretariat. His scholarly virtues and martial virtues glorified both ancient and modern times. Later, serving as the governor of Luoyang, he often recounted the story of the old men on Tianjin Bridge.

Duke Wei Qian Requested Rain During the period of Fang Chang, the magistrate of Jinyang County in the northern capital, Duke Wei Qian, who was a descendant of Liang Gong, held office with integrity and diligence, implementing efficient and orderly governance. He diligently pacified and protected the region, unafraid of powerful or intimidating forces. Under his jurisdiction, the state suffered from prolonged drought that spanned spring and summer, affecting hundreds of miles of water sources and farmland, all of which were wasted and dried up. People prayed at the Jin Temple for several weeks, but there were almost no signs of clouds or rain. At that time, there was a Taoist priest named Guo, who was originally a female shaman from Bingtu. She studied Taoist magic from a young age and practiced a method known as "yansheng." A military inspector was about to travel to the capital city, and due to her connection with a high-ranking official, she had access to the palace. Later, a military document was issued, and she returned home, taking the title of Master Taoist. The drought then became more severe, and the entire region was in distress, with no one knowing what to do. Everyone agreed that if the Master Taoist arrived at the Jin Temple, then the drought and disaster would not be a concern. Duke Wei Qian requested the commander to summon the Taoist priest, but the commander hesitated. Duke Wei Qian said, "Disaster and plague are spreading, and the common people are anxious. Without the intervention of the Master Taoist, the common people will likely be unable to survive." Therefore, the commander personally requested the shaman, who agreed and consented. Duke Wei Qian then prepared a carriage, arranged flags and canopies, and met her at his private residence, personally controlling the horse. Upon arriving at the temple, he set up elaborate offerings and prepared abundant food and drink. From morning until evening, he bowed deeply in reverence.

In the courtyard, where such things were happening, the next day, Guo said to Wei Qian, "I will fly a talisman to the upper realm for you to request rain. I have already received the command of the Heavenly Emperor. It must be done with utmost sincerity and earnestness. In three days, the rain will be sufficient." As a result, people from the four quarters ran and gathered, but after three nights, there was no sign of rain. Guo then said, "This land is where calamities arise, also due to the county magistrate's lack of virtue. I will again ascend to heaven to request. In seven days, there will be rain." Wei Qian took the blame upon himself and served with even greater respect. Soon it reached the expected time, yet there was no sign of rain. Guo suddenly demanded his horse and entered the prefectural residence. Wei Qian knelt and begged to keep him, saying, "The Master of Heaven has come for the benefit of all the people. I beg you to pray with utmost sincerity." Guo suddenly became angry and cursed, "You, an insignificant official, do not understand the ways of the world. The heavenly time is not willing to send rain. What good will it do to keep me here?" Wei Qian apologized, "I dare not trouble the Master of Heaven again. Wait until tomorrow morning, and I will arrange to send you off." Wei Qian then warned his attendants beforehand, "I have been insulted by the witch. How can I still speak of being an official? Tomorrow morning, I will give you other instructions. The right and wrong, the likes and dislikes, are the responsibility of the county magistrate." By dawn, the gate was still closed, and Guo had already prepared to return on horseback.

Regular offerings of food and wine were set up but went unused; the official sat in the imperial hall and freely scolded. Only Wei Qian said, "This female witch of the left path has long been deceiving and confusing the people; she must be killed today. How dare she speak of returning?" He ordered the attendants to seat her before the deity, struck her thirty times on the back, and cast her into the lake. Behind the shrine stood a mountain thousands of feet high; he immediately ordered a mat to be set up, incense to be burned, and all the attendants to be released, standing with their official insignia upon it. At once, the entire county was shocked and frightened, saying that the official had killed the heavenly teacher. Those fleeing scattered in confusion, while onlookers gathered in great numbers. At that time, a severe drought had lasted for many months, the heat so intense that stones were scorched and gold melted, the sky clear for thousands of miles with not a single cloud. Suddenly, a cloud like a carriage canopy appeared above the shrine, rising quickly and first covering the place where Wei Qian stood. The clouds of the four surrounding districts followed and converged. Several thunderclaps rang out, and then a great rain fell, bringing much-needed relief. The scorched earth and red fields were all refreshed. Then thousands of commoners and officials gathered from the mountain top and descended around Wei Qian. The governor had originally intended to kill the witch with a staff, and at first he was angry. But then, moved by her sincerity and devotion, he deeply admired and felt astonished. He submitted a report with the military commander to the court. Soon, an imperial edict praised and rewarded him with 500,000 coins, granted him official robes, and appointed him governor of the two states of Jiong and Xi. Under his governance, all were known for their good reputation.

Imperial Decree: Didi Weiqian, a man of noble lineage, talent, and loyalty, has witnessed this divine scourge that will afflict the common people. He should pray at the Jinci Temple, cast the witch into Yexian, expose himself to the terrifying sun, and perform acts equivalent to burning his body to summon dark, heavy clouds from the horizon. The method is the same as cutting one's nails, thereby causing the drought winds to cease and the sweet rain to flow again. The heart of heaven still recognizes his sincerity. My intention is not to forget his good deeds, but to specially grant him a red sash and a copper seal to shine, so that his good name is not forgotten and his extraordinary achievements are further highlighted. Wang Shizhong's Poem Wang Shizhong, known as Zhixing, is a man of extraordinary military strategy and talent. He was initially appointed as the governor of Xu Prefecture, where his great talent and noble character earned him a reputation in the world. Once the military office was established, all those appointed were scholars. He served in the mission office, where he drank and composed poetry leisurely. Soon, his poetry reached the ears of the Grand Master, who then summoned the guards and officials to gather. Wang Shizhong, however, dismissed the writing tools and simply welcomed them with cups and plates. After a long while, the Grand Master asked, "Just now I heard that the judge and the gentlemen were composing poetry, why did they stop when they saw me?" He immediately ordered them to take back the writing tools and scatter dozens of sheets of paper on the seat. The officials looked at each other hesitantly, waiting for the wine to be served and the music to begin. The Grand Master said again, "I wanted to see the creation, not to drink." At that time, a minor official also placed sheets of paper before the Grand Master. Wang Shizhong performed a courtesy bow. The Grand Master said, "Formerly, I rose to prominence through strategy and never paid attention to literary phrases. Today, accompanying the talented and wise, I cannot help but express my humble opinion." He then took up the brush and wrote it in a moment: "Throughout life, the bow and sword have been with me, and as a young official, I was ordered to write poetry. The flowers and willows of the south can be sung by you, but the dust of the north I know well." All present were amazed and exclaimed without end, all saying that the loyal and valiant words and colors are even more splendid than those of Cao Jingzong and He.

There is no way to surpass him. [Cao Jingzong composed a poem at the imperial court, which read: "When departing, the children are sad; when returning, the drums and horns are loud. Asking the passerby, how does it compare to Huo Qubing?" Emperor Wu of Liang was so pleased that he could not stop praising it. Another poem by the Sui general He Xianbi, given to Yuan Xiong, read: "The military camps of the River Jiao and the camp of the Pacifier of the South Sea. Do not let the Qilin ride without the names of the two of us." At the same time, the poet Zhang Hu also attended this banquet. The military governor said to him, "Seeing such a grand event, how could one remain silent?" Zhang Hu immediately composed a poem to present to him, which read: "From ancient times, the heroes have moved the world. In martial virtue and literary classics, there is none left. Outside the achievements of the Wang family, the writings of Li Ling and the calligraphy of Wang Xizhi." The general smiled and said, "Words of praise and embellishment can be said without holding back." Someone on the side said, "The students of the scholars are only engaged in flattery." The general scolded them, saying, "If someone says I dislike you, will you still do so?" Zhang Hu, a famous scholar of the Tang dynasty, was known for his poems. It was not easy to get such poems. When the people heard of it, they thought that General Wang Zhixing was a lover of good deeds. He stayed for several months, and presented him with a thousand bolts of silk as a gift. Later, he moved to Puzhen, and his son Yanping held a ceremonial staff in Lingwu. Many scholars from afar flocked to him, and his influence and legacy continued until today.]

The High Official and Taoist Monk Master Xiahou of Qiao Zhang the Attendant, serving as an assistant to the Governor of Heyang, Wu Sitan, gathered together with other officials. Among them was a Taoist monk named Yin Jiuxia, who had come from Mount Qingcheng and was known for his ability to discern the future. Wu Sitan asked him about the longevity and official rank of those present. Yin Jiuxia replied, "The Governor has reached the highest rank within the feudal system. What he aspires to is to hold a position of great power and to be granted a fiefdom, but this can only be achieved by conserving his achievements, embodying virtue and righteousness, and enjoying a life of great prosperity and happiness, which is without limit." Afterward, Wu Sitan asked Yin Jiuxia about the others. Yin Jiuxia said, "Among them, there must be someone who will become a high-ranking official." At that time, Wu Sitan held in high regard Pei the Deputy Commander. He asked, "Is Pei the Governor going to become a prime minister?" Yin Jiuxia replied, "Based on current events and human affairs, it should be as you say. But according to my observation, it is not so." At that moment, Xiahou the Prime Minister was serving as an inspector at the guesthouse. He appeared weak and unimpressive in physical appearance. Wu Sitan then joked, "Is it not Pei the Deputy Commander who will not become a prime minister, but rather Xiahou the Inspector?" The Deputy Commander replied, "What the Governor says is correct." Wu Sitan clapped his hands and laughed, "Could you be mistaken, Master?" Yin Jiuxia said, "I am a simple hermit who has long followed a righteous path and has no desire for fame, official positions, or material wealth. I speak based on what I see and hear, following a straightforward path." Wu Sitan said, "In that case, it is beyond my knowledge. But who is the next most important person?" Yin Jiuxia replied, "Zhang the Attendant, though he will not hold high office, will still achieve something significant."

After [榮顯] left, he went to visit Zhang the Attendant's residence and calmly said to him, "Your spirit and bones are clear and refreshing, and your aura and temperament are lofty and distant. However, you have been weighed down by ceremonial robes and hats for thirty to twenty years, living in the mundane world. If you can shed the noisy and vulgar customs and pursue the Dao together, you can achieve immortality within thirty years. My journey here is not driven by mundane concerns but is also to seek out those who cultivate the Dao. I have seen many people, but none like Zhang the Attendant. His words are vague and broad, but he only agrees with them. As I was about to leave, I returned, and my feelings were very sincere. Knowing Zhang's unwillingness, I deeply regretted it. Therefore, I left behind several pills and a sealed envelope containing a yellow document and departed, saying, 'These several pills, if taken, can keep you free of illness. The book records the gains from your official career, and every promotion, you secretly reveal it to yourself. The day the book ends, we will miss each other again.' Later, Zhang the Attendant indeed served in the court, going in and out of the imperial administration, wearing purple and red robes, and inspecting several states. The book records his words in great detail. By the time he was in his thirties, he was the Vice Minister of Revenue. What the book contains is also not much. Although he was prominent in name and position, his teeth and hair had declined. He often spoke about matters related to those who follow the Dao to his friends and relatives. Recalling his spirit, I could not match it."

In the spring of the ninth year of the Xiantong era (868 CE), a sudden and violent storm with thunder and lightning occurred about ten li south of Huayin County. A dragon moved a body of water from afar, and it came to rest in a place where there were high and low hills and ridges, which was not a suitable location for storing water. The pond was clear and undulating, as if it were artificially dug out. Travelers from the capital (Dongjing or Luoyang) took detours to see this miraculous event. People interested in the event traveled from the capital and other places, and the area was continuously crowded with carriages and horses for several months. Near the temple, there was a pond called San Nangzi Pond, which was close to the cliffs. The water was clear and transparent, and its depth was impossible to measure. Every autumn, when the wind caused the leaves to fall, there were never any leaves or stems floating on the surface of the pond. If anyone saw a small leaf or thin stem, it was immediately taken away by birds flying away. People who prayed often offered cosmetics, decorative items, and fine silk garments, which were then thrown into the pond and disappeared suddenly. At the beginning of the Ganfu era (874-879 CE), several officials visited Mount Zhongnan and came to the miraculous pond. They discussed the miraculous events associated with the deity. Among them, one official did not believe in the event and threw wood and stones into the pond. Suddenly, a large fish jumped out of the water, with scales like snow. There was also a sudden heavy rainstorm, and the carriages and horses were nearly swept away by the flood. After this event, people became even more reverent, and nobody dared to violate the pond.

Emperor Wenzong of Tang often kept a white jade pillow, a treasure of rare times, given by the kingdom of Yutian during the reign of Emperor Dezong, in his bedchamber. One day, it suddenly went missing. The palace guards were strict, and only those favored by the emperor or his concubines could access the bedchamber. Other treasures were displayed, but none were lost. The emperor was shocked and alarmed for a while, then issued an edict in the capital to search for the thief. He secretly met with the chief eunuchs and other high-ranking officials and said, "This is not an external invasion; the thief must be within the palace grounds. If we do not find the thief, we fear other changes. One pillow is truly not worth regretting, but you all guard my palace and must surely catch the criminal. Otherwise, the circle of the emperor's attendants will be useless from now on." The eunuchs were terrified and apologized, requesting to search for ten days and offering a large reward in gold and silk. However, there was no trace to be found. The emperor's decree was strict, and the number of people being detained increased. Every street and alley was searched. There was a general of the Dragon and Tiger divisions named Wang Jinghong who usually kept a young servant, just eighteen or nineteen years old, graceful and agile, whom he sent everywhere. Wang Jinghong once accompanied his peers to a banquet at the Wei Yuan military camp, where there was a young woman skilled at playing a Huqin (a type of fiddle). When everyone was drunk, she then requested to perform a song.

Jing Hong was about to play the pipa, but it was not played well unless it was handled by a skilled player. The clock had already indicated the time, and it was too late to fetch the instrument. He got up and loosened his belt. The servant said, "The pipa can be brought in an instant." Jing Hong replied, "The guard drums were just beating, and the military gates were already locked. You should have known this; what you said is wrong." Afterward, they began drinking several rounds. The servant arrived with the pipa in a brocade bag. The guests laughed and said, "Instruments usually accompany people, but the difficulty lies in finding a skilled player." The southern army was thirty miles away from the left wing, and at night there was no formation or order. Soon after, there was sudden back-and-forth movement. Jing Hong was surprised and lost track of time. The servant apologized, saying that he had no such thing but could travel quickly. He then explained that his parents were in Sichuan, and he had recently come to the capital and now wanted to return home. He wanted to repay a debt of gratitude; he knew the name of the thief and would make them confess within a few days. Jing Hong said, "Such a matter is not trivial and has led to many people being saved, but the identity of the thief is still unknown."

How can the thief be reported to the guard? The minor servant said, "The thief of the pillow is Tian Penglang. He serves in the military ranks, his behavior is unpredictable, he has extraordinary bravery and strength, and he is good at leaping over obstacles. If we do not wait for an opportunity to break his leg, even with a thousand soldiers and horsemen, he will flee. From now on for two nights, we should wait for him at the Gate of the Immortal View, and we will certainly capture him when the opportunity arises." The general followed him and observed the situation. This matter still needed to be kept secret. At that time, it had been dry for over ten days, and in the morning, the dust was very thick, making it impossible to see people even though they were on horseback. Penglang and several young men linked arms and were about to enter the military gate. The minor servant hit him with a ball stick, and suddenly, his left foot was broken. Penglang looked up at him and said, "I stole the pillow, I don't fear others, only you." After that, when they met again, there was no need for more words. Therefore, he was carried to the left army and confessed immediately. The emperor was pleased to have captured the thief and knew he was captured by the imperial guards. He brought Penglang to the hall for questioning, and Penglang confessed that he often came and went in the palace. The emperor said, "This is a type of wandering knight, not a common thief. There are hundreds of prisoners inside and outside, so all were pardoned." The minor servant initially told Jinghong to return to Shu, but it was impossible to find him, so only Jinghong was rewarded.

In the autumn of the fourth year of Xiantong, a great flood struck Luoyang, submerging all the gardens, pavilions, and houses. Afterward, the monks of Xiangshan Temple reported that as the day was drawing to a close, they saw a sudden flood rushing down from the north of Longmen Creek, as if the rivers and seas had burst their banks. In the midst of its fury, it roared like thunder. Two black oxen emerged from the water, swinging their tails and leaping through the air. The monks and residents looked down from a high place and thought the entire city had turned into fish. Soon they saw the two gates of Dinding and Changxia suddenly open, and there was also a blue ox leaping out. They were about a hundred steps apart. The black oxen ran away, and the previously raging waves and surges suddenly subsided several zhang. That night, the flooding was especially severe, and the capital city became desolate. For over ten years, it had not been fully repaired. Before this, a guard at the imperial city heard someone singing in the Wu Feng Tower during the day: "Firelight appeared on the banks of the Tianjin Bridge; watching the floodwaters on the Wei Wang Di." At that time, Chancellor Vang Yai was stationed in Luoyang, and he heard it and thought it was a delusion. After more than a month, the officials returned home after a banquet. The person holding the candle dropped some embers, and as soon as the riders passed, the smoke and flames rose high. It was too late to save it, and half of it was burned. When the floodwaters began to rise, the Gu and Luo rivers rose first, and the Wei Wang Di and Yuebo Di both broke. Thus, the guard's words were proven true.

Li Zhuyi, the Righteous and Wise Duke, Li Bai, the Middle Secretary, was stationed in the Langshu office, and no one knew him. Only the Righteous and Wise Duke of Zhuji admired him and spread his reputation among the scholars. However, his resources were insufficient to support his colleagues. One day, the Duke left him ten thousand coins to prepare for food and drink, and invited several distinguished scholars from the Shengge to visit his residence on a specific day. It was late autumn, and the weather had been gloomy and rainy for over ten days. Heba Ji, an official outside the capital, had failed to obtain a position and intended to take a brief journey. He had passed the imperial examination in the same year as Li Bai and was about to leave when he arrived at the gate to say goodbye. The gatekeeper, who was waiting for officials, told him that they were elsewhere. Heba Ji then stopped his carriage and left a letter, fully describing his intention of being on a journey. Li Bai read the letter and exclaimed, "A man in life, his poverty or wealth is determined by fate. If he is unworthy and gains favor by chance, it is not a proper way to advance. How can he keep beautiful things at home only to invite powerful officials? In the past, when we passed the imperial examination together, we were poor friends, but today, he has closed his door and does not meet. Even if he has no honor or fame, how can he not feel ashamed? I immediately ordered the servant to have Heba Ji turn back his carriage, and then we shared the food and drink. Soon, the invited officials arrived, riding together, and the gatekeeper reported in detail.

抜惎从容无不惋愕而去。翌日，于私第谒见相国，询朝士来者为谁。白公对以宾客未至，适有同年出京访别，悯其龙钟委困，不忍弃之，留饮数杯，遂阙祗接。既负吹嘘之际，甘从谴斥之罪。相国称赏逾时，云此事真古人之道，由兹贵达，所以激劝浇薄。不旬月，自使下评事，先授美官。白公以库部郎中入为翰林学士，未逾三载，便秉钧衡。其后五镇藩维，再居廊庙，蹈义怀仁，而终始一致，流芳传素，士林美之。大中初，边鄙不宁，土蕃尤甚，恣其倔强。宣宗欲致讨伐，遂于延英殿先问宰臣，公首奏兴师，请为统帅，防边藩镇兵士数万，鼓行而前。时犬戎临阵平川，以生骑数千伏藏山谷，既而得于谍者，遂设。

Awaiting the enemy with an unexpected force, a high-ranking leader from a foreign tribe appeared, wearing a red fur coat and a belt adorned with precious ornaments, riding an exceptionally swift and rare white steed. Before the weapons had clashed, he whipped his horse out onto the battlefield multiple times, repeatedly challenging the Han army's generals to fight. General Bai warned his soldiers not to respond. Soon, he halted his troops and stood facing us, about a hundred steps away. A brave and skilled archer from the state of Lu requested a fast horse and drew his bow, riding out. He fired two arrows in succession, both hitting the leader's neck. He leaped forward on his horse, drew his short sword, and fell to the ground on the saddle, holding himself up with his hands as if engaged in battle. The soldiers only shouted and cheered him on. He then removed his red fur coat, took off his golden belt, and rode back on the enemy's horse. The entire army was inspired to fight bravely. After a great battle in the desert, the enemy's formation collapsed like a landslide. We pursued the fleeing enemy almost to the foot of Heishan Mountain. The spoils of war, including camels, horses, and supplies, were too numerous to count. About three or four thousand people surrendered. Prior to this, the regions of Hexi and Guanzhong were under the control of the Xiongnu, but from now on they became part of the interior. When Emperor Xuan first read the report, he said, "I knew that Mingzhong would surely destroy the wicked." When General Bai returned victorious, he and the other chancellors presented him with a poem saying, "One edict from the imperial capital was sent throughout the empire." The enemy forces were vanquished.

A Poem of Peace and Prosperity Soldiers return to their watchtowers, the flutes signaling a cessation of war. In the wilderness, horses neigh in the wind, and the river winds through the mountains, locking the passes for a thousand miles. The western and northern borders are now peaceful, and news is brought from the eastern and southern regions of the submission of the frontier tribes. Ancient poetry speaks of the virtues of Emperor Shun and Emperor Yao, who tamed the distant peoples of the Dog and Qiang tribes. The emperor's benevolent rule and martial prowess have been celebrated, and the expansion of the empire is seen as a great achievement of a sage ruler. The four generals have been successful in their campaigns without heavy fighting, and the seven passes, though still guarded, have seen a reduction in military tension. The current state of peace and prosperity is due to the wisdom and leadership of a great ruler, supported by the counsel of wise ministers like Wei Zheng. The recent recovery of the Xiangguan region has been marked by clear skies and the submission of the enemy, influenced by the emperor's benevolent rule and growing power. The peace and prosperity have spread far and wide, with the emperor's martial prowess celebrated in history. The people on the eastern frontier know the emperor's benevolence and no longer need to prepare for defense. The poem continues to praise the emperor's rule and the resulting peace and prosperity, emphasizing the submission of the enemy, the celebration of the emperor's rule, and the harmony of the times.

**The Collected Conversations, Volume 1** **Authorized by the Emperor, the Four Treasuries of Books** **The Collected Conversations, Volume 2** Compiled by Kang Bian of the Tang Dynasty **The Residence of the Duke of Tongyi in Tongyi Lane** This residence was the former home of Li Jinxian, the military governor of Shaofang during the reign of Emperor Wenzong. Li Jinxian rose from military service and was distinguished and remarkable, having held several positions as a frontier governor. He was wealthy in wealth and treasures, living a luxurious and extravagant life, and had a refined taste for entertaining guests. He often spoke of his days when he had experienced setbacks while traveling on the borders, and Li Jinxian welcomed him with great enthusiasm. Later, when they met in the capital, they also visited his residence. When the peony flowers were in full bloom, he celebrated the peony flowers. On the appointed day, they went there, and preparations were made in advance. The food and drink at the banquet were not ordinary. After several rounds of toasting, he led the guests into the inner chambers. The rooms were magnificent, with beams and pillars adorned with brocade. The banquet tables were extensive, and all the utensils were made of gold. Before the steps, there were several clusters of flowers covered with brocade canopies. The concubines and maidservants wore fine silk and satin, and there were many who were skilled in music and dance. Each guest had two young female servants with double buns who attended to all their needs. The service was so attentive that even the usual attendants were not as good as the exquisite wine and dishes, which were the best of land and sea. As for the servants, carriages, and supplies, everything was abundant. From noon until the next morning, there was no sign of the dishes being left in disarray. The court officials said that after observing this, they saw the splendor of the wealthy and powerful.

Those who attain such a high position in their social circles are very rare. Later, Xian moved to Changxing, and his house alternately became the property of others. During the year 864 of the Tang Dynasty, Liu, the chancellor, was dismissed from his post as deputy governor of Beijing and then appointed as a scholar in the Hanlin Academy. A few years later, he entered the position of chancellor after serving as a defense official and still paid a tax of 10,000 coins on the property. When he was sent to govern Jingnan, the court and the people all felt great regret. The people of the capital, from young to old, all heard of it and wept. Later, Xiao, a chancellor, was promoted to a high position and nominated as a representative. Righteous people silently suppressed their anger and clenched their fists, while the common people shed tears in the streets. At that time, Zheng, a chancellor, in the inner court, drafted the edict at night, detailing the matter. He said that a small house of a few acres was still not owned by him, and he refused gifts from all directions, only fearing that others would know. At that time, it was widely copied in the capital, and the powerful and influential people were greatly angered by the words. Zheng was demoted to the governor of Wuzhou from the Hanlin Academy, and the chancellor was exiled to Rizhao from Duanxi. Exiled for four years, he finally cleared of the charges and was recalled to serve as the Secretary of the Secretariat. Not long after, he regained his high position. Or offered his grand mansion as a gift, but ultimately refused to accept it. He returned to live in this house, and the courtyard and buildings were not renovated. His integrity and frugality filled the world.

The wise say that the influence of benevolence and righteousness on the world is great indeed. Duke Liu understands the way of the emperor and is sagely enlightened, wishing that all virtuous people come forward, and the entire world unites in following his lead. Although exiled for many years, he was once again reinstated to a high position. The mouths of the wicked cannot conceal his virtue, and the realm of evil spirits cannot trap his body. He gains fame in his time and leaves a lasting fragrance for a thousand years. Is this not great? How great a mistake it is for those who assume office titles, receive bribes, rely on favor to seize power, and live in the flesh but die in reputation! **Li Xiangguo's Residence** The residence of Li Xiangguo of Zhu Ya is located in the southeastern corner of the Anyi Ward. Sang Daomao referred to it as the Jade Bowl, as the dwelling is not particularly grand but is uniquely ingenious in its design. Among its features are strange stones and ancient pines, which resemble paintings. During the reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Wu, Li Xiangguo wielded immense authority and influence, unmatched in power and favor. He often sought out rare and extraordinary items, and those who sought his favor frequently offered precious treasures. Once, during a day of leisure, he invited his fellow chancellors and court officials to a banquet. At the time, the intense sunlight caused everyone to suffer from the heat. The carriages and umbrellas had arrived by noon, and the scholars and gentlemen were fanning themselves frantically, hoping to find a cool place to rest.
