They were then invited to a small study, which was not particularly spacious but had walls adorned with ancient books and famous paintings. Despite the heat, they soon felt a refreshing breeze, as if they were in the cool autumn air. A full banquet was prepared, and they continued until dusk. When they left, the blazing sun and scorching heat were still intense. A curious person asked a trusted servant about it, who replied that on that day, only a basin of water soaked in white dragon skin was placed at the end of the seat. [The dragon skin was obtained by a monk from Silla from the sea.]

A Resident by the Sea Obtained Something from a Fisherman By the sea, there lived a resident who obtained something from a fisherman. Initially, he thought it to be a type of fish or shellfish. An old man saw it and recognized it. The monk, knowing that the chancellor was curious, redeemed it with gold and silk and presented it to him. The items included warm gold bracelets, dust-proof hairpins, all rare treasures of the age. When they moved south, all were lost in the bad stream. Even if one sends a Kunlun (a mythical figure) to dive into the water to search for it in the crocodile's den, it cannot be retrieved. Pingquan Villa is thirty miles from Luoyang. The villa is adorned with flowers, trees, pavilions, and terraces, like a fairyland. There is a veranda with a stream flowing through it, winding and cut, resembling the twelve peaks of Ba Gorge, the nine rivers of Dongting Lake, and the sea gate, all with the shapes of clouds, rainbows, dragons, phoenixes, grass, and trees. There is a large fish rib, one long twenty-five feet, with an inscription on it reading, "Sent from Hai Prefecture in the sixth year of Fanchang (660 AD)." It was found in the southeast corner of the villa. This was the villa of the recluse Wei Chulao, a poet known for his high-spirited and refined temperament and his love for natural landscapes. The chancellor, who had risen from a civilian position to a high office, later returned to Pingquan Villa and visited the villa. Wei Chulao, however, avoided him by hiding in the mountains. The chancellor then wrote a poem: "Once summoned by the imperial decree, I was ashamed to be in the phoenix pool. Now I come to seek the recluse, but alas, I cannot see the jade branches."

The residence of the nobleman Niu, located on the north street of Xinchang, was originally the estate of Kang Xi, the supervisor of construction during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. Sang Daomao called it the "Golden Cup," as both places produced virtuous ministers. The Peony of Cien Temple In the early morning of the capital city, the peony is especially esteemed among flowers. As for Buddhist temples and Taoist shrines, visitors rarely fail to visit. In the bathing hall of Cien Temple, there are two clusters of flowers. When they bloom, they produce hundreds of blossoms, each one richly colored and fragrant, nearly unparalleled. There was a monk named Si Zhen who often spoke of a group of officials from the Tang Dynasty who sought beauty and visited the temple. At the time, in the east corridor, there was a white flower that was particularly lovely. They gathered to drink wine and sit together, commenting that the abundance of peonies was indeed remarkable. However, the world's admiration is usually limited to light red and deep purple hues, and no one has ever seen a deeper shade of red. The head monk, an elderly monk, smiled and said, "How could there not be such a flower? It is just that you gentlemen have not seen it." They then questioned him, and he stayed the night. He said, "The words I spoke earlier must be true, as I have seen it before. I hope you will lead me to see it, and my wish for spring outings will be fulfilled." The monk merely said, "I once saw it in another place, far from the imperial capital. By dawn, they continued to seek it, and the monk finally revealed, "If you gentlemen are so fond of it, how could I keep it hidden? Today, I wish to show it to you together, but I do not know if you will keep it a secret." The officials took an oath, saying, "We swear to never speak of it again." The monk then opened a room, where there were banners and images set up, with a wooden partition covered by an old curtain.

A pavilion was opened, and they entered a courtyard with two small halls, both very ornate and splendid, with eaves, corridors, and railings all made of cypress wood. There was a deep red peony bush, sprawling with nearly a thousand blooms. The early morning sun just began to dry the dew on the flowers, and the peony's rich and full appearance, partially open, dazzled the eyes. The officials were amazed and delighted, lingering until dusk before leaving. The monk said, "I have cherished and cultivated this peony for nearly twenty years. Now, I have spoken of it, and people have seen it. From now on, I do not know what will happen." Two nights later, several influential young men and their friends came together to the temple. They entered the hall with the peony, spending a long time there in a relaxed manner. The monk led them to Qujiang, where they took a leisurely walk. As they were about to leave, he asked a servant to place his tea chest, wrapped in a yellow cloth, on the grass by the Qujiang shore. Suddenly, a disciple ran to them, saying that a group of people had entered the garden and were digging up the flowers, despite attempts to stop them. The monk bowed his head and remained silent, only sighing to himself. The guests looked at each other and laughed. Soon after, they returned to the temple gate, where they saw the flowers being carried away in large baskets. The people who took the flowers then said to the monk, "We knew your garden had a famous peony, and everyone wanted to see it. We did not inform you beforehand, fearing it would be difficult to see it. The gift in the chest I just left contains thirty taels of gold and two catties of Sichuan tea as a token of appreciation."

Guan Wandi Encounters a Martial Hero Guan Wandi, a brave warrior from the left division of the Fang Chang army, was renowned for his extraordinary physical strength, capable of lifting a tripod and carrying a yoke. He was respected and feared by all. One day, while drinking with his companions in a tavern in the eastern market, a man dressed in coarse clothing and holding an umbrella suddenly entered their seat and took up a drinking vessel to drink, with no one around. Guan Wandi, shaking his wrist and glaring, showed no fear or hesitation. His companions, who were also brave, tried to push and pull him, but he did not move slightly. As the number of onlookers grew, the man said, "I will test my strength against Guan Wendi to determine who is stronger. First, I will ask Guan Wendi to punch me three times, then I will deliver a single blow to him." He then stood with his arms around a pillar. Enraged by the man's audacity, Guan Wendi wanted to defeat him and struck with all his might, hitting like wood or stone. The onlookers saw the pillar and the building shake, but the man did not move slightly. He then smiled and said, "It is now my turn to deliver a blow to Guan Wendi." With a powerful arm, he rose, his palm as large as a basket, and descended with a force that reached over ten feet, standing firm. Those who were strong were greatly frightened and recognized that the man was no ordinary person. They gathered around Guan Wendi, who apologized and left. The man suddenly disappeared. Guan Wendi fell ill for over a month, and his strength gradually diminished.

In the era of Xianzong (Salty Tongue), there was a man named Zhang Jihong from the Left Army, who was brave and strong. Once in the rain, he passed through Shengye Lane and encountered deep and narrow mud. A villager was driving a donkey carrying firewood and happened to be in the way. Zhang Jihong became angry and grabbed the donkey by its four feet, throwing it over a water channel several steps away. All spectators were amazed and frightened. Later, serving as an attendant in Xiangzhou, he stopped for the night at a guesthouse in Shangshan. At the guesthouse, an old woman told her son, "The evil man is returning. Quickly prepare food and tea, and do not let him be disturbed." Earlier, I saw the old woman worried. What is the matter? Is the new wife unable to speak?" Zhang Jihong asked. The old woman replied, "The guest does not know other things. But I will use my strength to help the master. The mother and son quickly bowed, saying, 'If you can help us, we will be free of worry, although we are poor.'"

The woman was expected to return a gift. Soon, all the neighbors and fellow villagers came to watch. As dusk fell, the woman returned home carrying a bundle of firewood, and her appearance showed no other abnormalities. At the inn, there was a garden with a large flat stone. Season Hong sat on it, placing his horsewhip beside him. He called out to her and said, "You are the new wife of the owner. I heard in Chang'an that you rely on your strength and do not submit to your husband. How dare you act this way?" The woman bowed to Season Hong and said, "Please, Sir, do not be hasty. Allow me to explain. I dare not fail to serve my husband, but since then, the master has been displeased with me." The old woman standing nearby said, "Do not speak nonsense in front of the guest." The woman then said, "For example, on a certain day and month, such an event occurred. How could it be my fault? For every matter I mention, I draw on the stone where Season Hong is sitting with my middle finger, and it leaves a deep mark several inches long." Season Hong was sweating profusely and was terrified, but he only said that the reasoning was correct. He closed the door, pretended to sleep, and waited until morning to leave. When he later inquired about the woman, she had already gone elsewhere.

The Immortal of the Jade Fragrance Courtyard Descended At the Tang Chang Temple in Anyei Ward of the Capital City, there were once many jade fragrance flowers, which bloomed like a forest of jade and crystal. During the Yuanhe period, when spring was in full bloom, carriages and horses came one after another to enjoy the scenery. Suddenly, one day, a girl around seventeen or eighteen years old appeared. She was wearing a green brocade robe, riding a horse, with an elaborate hairstyle and two buns, without any hairpins or earrings. Her appearance was graceful and stood out among the crowd. Accompanied by two female priests and three female servants, all dressed in yellow robes and looking extremely beautiful, she dismounted and used a white horn fan to shield her face as she went straight to the flower spot. An unusual fragrance filled the air, reaching dozens of steps away. The onlookers thought she came from the imperial palace and dared not approach her. She stood there for a long time, then ordered a young servant to pick several branches of flowers and leave. As she was about to ride away, she said to the priest in the yellow robe, "Now the agreement made at Jade Peak can be fulfilled." At that time, the crowd was like a wall, and everyone felt the presence of smoke and birds, with the scenery shining brightly. She rode a hundred steps, with a light wind carrying the dust away as she left. After a while, the dust disappeared, and looking up, she was already halfway to the sky. It was then realized that it was a journey of the immortals. The lingering fragrance lasted for more than a month.

Yan Geshi, Liu Yuxi, and Bai Juyi all wrote poems about the immortal descending from the Jade Pear Blossom Garden. Yan Geshi's poem says: "In inner cultivation and prayer, the spirit fades and the eyes grow cold; not meeting the true one is not as good as facing the jade pear blossoms and laughing with the inhabitants of the flower cave." He also says: "The feathered carriage descends from the Jade Turtle Mountain; in the mortal world, one cannot see the beauty of the immortal; only the snow on the branches, blown by the wind, weaves through the green hair of the immortal." Liu Yuxi's poem says: "The immortal maiden passes through the jade tree without allowing the green bird to emerge from the flower branches; surely no one else has noticed, only Yan Geshi could predict this." He also says: "The immortal maid looks at the jade tree flowers; the fragrant car leads the way; she plays with the snow and turns her head, amazed at how quickly time passes in the mortal world." Bai Juyi's poem says: "The immortal maiden steals a ride on a phoenix; in the cave, she rests and plays with the jade branches; not because the birds sing too much in spring, the secluded immortal can know." The Chancellor's alms.

During the Dagan period, a prime minister returned home from the Zhongshu Sheng and had someone carry thousands of coins in a cloth bag to distribute on the road to give to beggars. As a result, the poor gathered along the roadside. However, the distribution became too small to reach everyone, and the prime minister's entourage lost its dignity and decorum. At that time, an official submitted a memorial to advise against this practice. The main points were as follows: Nowadays, with frequent wars and the people's strength depleted, we hope the great minister will help perfect the great transformation, establish and maintain the norms, promote the virtuous, and appoint the capable, to illuminate all matters and ensure that all things are in their proper order. The common people do not know about the reduction of unnecessary officials and the blocking of private requests. If this is done, then punishments will be clear, customs will be rich, and there will be no destitute people in the world. It is not appropriate to monopolize power in the government while only performing small favors. The wise men said that not knowing how to govern is worse than not building bridges in time. The prime minister read the memorial and became angry; shortly after, the rebels occupied the capital, and he met his downfall. Cui Daoshu, the son-in-law of Wei Yanzizi, who was a mid-level official in the Chancellery, failed the imperial examination in the spring of the second year of the Dagan period and returned to his hometown in Hanzhong. At his home, a carp with a golden scale and a fierce gaze, about five feet long, was found in the well. He ordered his servant to throw it into the river. Cui Daoshu and his cousin Wei prepared a feast and cooked the fish to eat it. After two nights, Wei fell ill and died suddenly. A man in green clothes led him to a grand and solemn mansion. Upon entering, he saw a woman wearing a gold and jade crown and a purple embroidered robe sitting at a desk. Her attendants wore yellow robes and gold combs, similar to those in the palace. A clerk carrying a ledger came out and placed it on the woman's desk. The clerk then led Wei to an eastern hall where he was questioned about the killing of the fish. Wei blamed Cui Daoshu, saying it was not his fault. The clerk said, "This matter will be investigated further."

The Rain Dragon lies hidden in rivers, seas, and marshy places; even if humans eat it, there is no way to distinguish it. But yesterday, it was caught in a well. Cui's family and you are not foolish; killing and eating it would both be difficult to escape. Still, you should return and try to perform Buddhist and Daoist good deeds with Cui Guang, perhaps to reduce the sin. From now on, every ten days, it will call again. Wei suddenly woke up and told all that was said to his relatives, ordering Dao Chu to recount the story in detail. Dao Chu was deeply worried but did not fully believe. Only a few days later, Wei died. Wei is the son of Dao Chu's aunt. A few days later, Wei sent a dream to his mother, saying, "Killed a fish and committed a sin, the place I went to was the Water Realm. Soon I will receive a severe punishment. You can urgently perform the Yellow Scripture Daoist ritual, still hoping to receive lenient punishment. The sin of my brother-in-law is also complete. Tonight I will know the matter myself." Wei's mother wept and told Dao Chu. At midnight, she woke up dazed and weeping, not knowing what to do. The season was winter, and his mother was just performing the Great Blessing ritual. Only a few days into spring, he fell ill and died. At that time, Cui's wife and children were all in the capital. Ziwei recounted the matter in detail. [According to old legends, Fangzhou and Niuzhuji are both the Water Realm, but it is unclear where Dao Chu went.]

During the Danyu period in Luoyang, there were wealthy and influential young men who inherited privileges and honors due to their family connections. They lived lives of luxury, with abundant material possessions and fine clothes and delicious food, not being cautious about overindulgence. They drank fine and rare beverages and satisfied all their desires related to food and drink. There was a former official named Li who returned from his term as an administrator and also resided in Eastern Luoyang. Deeply grateful for past favors, he wanted to invite his sons for a leisurely gathering. He frequently visited a monk named Shenggang at the Jing'ai Temple. Li consulted the monk about the behavior of the young men, saying that they often ate a variety of delicacies from both land and sea and cooked meals with charcoal, but frequently did not meet their expectations. This was a habit of arrogance and indulgence. Li asked if inviting them was advisable. The monk replied that rare and exotic delicacies might be difficult to obtain, but preparing a refined and clean small banquet was not a difficult task. Li then searched for rare and valuable items and instructed his wife and children to personally prepare the dishes. He set up beautifully decorated tables and plates and invited his brothers for a meal. The brothers sat formally, as if made of ice and jade, but despite the abundance of food, they rarely touched their chopsticks. The host repeatedly invited them to eat, but they only ate fruits. When it came to the water dishes, they took a small spoonful of each, but looked at each other for a long time, feeling as if eating bitter herbs and bitter roots. Li did not understand the reason and apologized for the unsuccessful meal. The next day, he prepared another banquet.

Revisiting the detailed description of the disciples' demeanor by Saint Gang, the monk said, "Could my previous statement be mistaken?" He then went to visit Saint Gang's residence to ask him, "Master Li specially prepared a feast; the cooking methods were not perfect. Why did you not accept the invitation?" The disciples replied, "The roasting, grilling, and cooking methods have not been perfected." The monk said, "Other things, even if they cannot be eaten, are cooked with charcoal; what is there to complain about the rice?" They said, "Master, you do not know that when cooking rice with charcoal, it must first be burned until it is fully cooked, which is called 'refined fire.' Only then can it be placed in the stove. Otherwise, there will still be smoke." The disciples explained that in Master Li's household, the charcoal was not properly pre-cooked, which made the food difficult to eat. The monk clapped his hands and laughed, "This is not something I know." Later, during a great invasion, the cities of Cai and Luo were captured, and their properties were looted. Several brothers and Saint Gang fled together, hiding in the mountains for three days without food. When the invaders moved further away, they continued their journey to the river bridge. Along the way, a small shop opened, selling coarse millet as food. The monk had a few coins in his bag and bought a meal in a clay cup, eating it with the group. Despite their severe hunger, they found the fine food less appealing than the coarse millet. The monk jokingly said, "This is not cooked with refined charcoal; do you think the gentlemen can eat it?" The disciples lowered their heads in shame and had no words to respond.

As the ancients say, the nature of those raised on rich food is hard to correct; is this what they mean? Therefore, the wise measure their food according to their capacity, and the virtuous guard against luxury and ease. Emperor Song Wu visited the Wu Zhan Hall and ordered his sons and grandsons not to eat. Upon reaching the guard post, he granted them food late in the day, and they all had hungry looks. The emperor said to them, "You young and old are proud and wealthy, and you do not see the hardships of the people. Now I make you understand the suffering of hunger and know how to expect others to be frugal." The sages of old had the same concern. The Fengxiang Commandery raised troops to fight the rebels. The rebel Nest attacked the palace gates, and the feudal lords near the capital had no military preparations. [Initially, Wang Xianzhi suffered a defeat, and Huang Fang led the remnants south to Jiaoguang. The court appointed Gao Pian to lead the armies of various circuits to occupy strategic points along the Yangtze and Huai rivers. Some argued that Gao Pian was a famous general of many generations and could control the rebels from his seat. When the rebels gathered and came north, they sailed and approached the Huai region, setting up camps widely in Tianchang County. Gao Pian did not send out troops, only closing the gates and protecting himself. The rebels' momentum became even sharper, advancing and crossing the Huai River northward. At that time, the Prime Minister of Qi governed Qingzhou, seeing the strong and numerous rebels like bees and ants, and also did not dare to advance, quickly withdrawing his banners and retreating. Some lingered on the way. The feudal lords had no defense, and the court again lost its strategy. Bandits roamed the nine avenues without being noticed, how strange is this?] At that time, the Chancellor of Li governed Puzhen, and Duke Zheng governed Qishan. After hearing about the relocation of the imperial court, both had the thought of serving the king. Duke Zheng led his officials, officers, and soldiers to patrol the city walls, inspecting the city walls and moats thoroughly.

All efforts to destroy and block the construction were put in place, and the calculation of the labor and time required was that it would take more than a month for ten thousand troops to complete the work. However, the enemy forces were at their peak strength, and a sudden and unexpected attack was imminent. The next morning, the commander-in-chief was called back and seated inside the defensive position. He was consulted for a plan, and everyone agreed that the large-scale bandits were at their height and it was not advisable to engage them directly. The majority wanted to adopt a temporary measure and wait for the troops to gather before planning to retake the lost territory. The chancellor asked if the generals were suggesting that he should submit to the enemy. Suddenly, he fell down. Those around him could not catch him before he was injured by the ground's uneven surface, causing his face and head to be bruised. By noon the next day, he still could not speak. At that time, all the military commanders in the Guan area had surrendered, except for Fengxiang, which had no news. The enemy was discussing raising an army to attack, and someone who fled brought the news. The military governor and his subordinates wrote a memorial and sent two horsemen to the capital. After reading the memorial, the enemy was very pleased and sent Wang Huishun with a large number of people and valuable gifts to express gratitude and comfort. They prepared a banquet to welcome Wang Huishun, and the banquet setup was as grand as before. Everyone sat down, and the wine was served; the musicians were about to start playing.

When the military and civil officials, as well as the musicians and entertainers, all cried out in grief at the same time, the official overseeing the military wiped away his tears to stop them. After a long time, they finally calmed down. Huai Shun and those who came were all shocked and looked at each other in surprise. They then asked the reason. The chancellor, who had been governing this region, had extended his benevolence to all things. In his spare time from governing, he occasionally ordered music to enjoy the company of the officials. Suddenly, due to illness, he did not attend this gathering today. The people, hearing the music, couldn't help but cry. The entire city's elderly and young were all filled with sorrow. The chancellor said, "I know that the hearts of the people have not yet tired of the virtue of the Tang dynasty. Although the rebels are powerful and have seized the palace, their downfall will come soon. I have secretly sent out urgent messages to inform the neighboring regions. Within a few days, defensive troops from various regions and border garrisons gathered with elite forces. Shortly after, the imperial guards also arrived upon hearing the news. Under my command, one day, the rebels sent over a thousand people to thoroughly search for food supplies. At that time, the watchtowers were already on high alert, and the patrols were especially strict. The flags and sharp weapons stood in an orderly and imposing manner."

Within a hundred miles, when the bandits entered the boundary, everyone was greatly alarmed and all were captured and executed. Those at the rear ran to report the situation, and the enemy was terrified. The capital was captured, and the common people also thought to fight bravely. [Initially, someone ran back to the capital and reported that Zheng, the chancellor of Fengxiang, had rebelled against the Yellow Emperor with a large army. The leader heard of this and was not at all concerned, saying, "My actions have already occupied half of the world, and wherever I go, I conquer without fail. Even Gao, the commander, still dared not send out troops to oppose me. What plans is Zheng making? Only Zhao, Zhang, Wang, and Pan had already made preparations to flee."] Soon after, the regions of Pu, Guan, Jin, Jie, Fen, Ze, and Lu, as well as the three towns of Hebei, raised a mighty army and unanimously rallied. The chancellor held the staff of authority, trained the troops, raised the flag, swore the troops, broke through the enemy's sharpness, supervised the battle, repeatedly defeated the enemy's leaders, used ingenious strategies, and finally destroyed the great evil. Later, upon the request of the court of Yongshu, the chancellor again held the power of transformation, reformed the state, and helped the times, consistent from beginning to end, with literary and martial strategies, how could they be inferior to ancient people? The poem says, "The mountains produce divine beings, giving birth to Fu and Shen. Fu and Shen were the pillars of Zhou, and the chancellor was among them."

Laojun Temple Paintings In the eastern capital, on the north side of Mount Beimang, there is the Xuan Yuan Temple, and to the south of it stands the Laojun Temple. The temple's halls and pavilions are grand and spacious, overlooking the Yi and Luo rivers. The clay statues of immortals and deities, all crafted during the Kaiyuan era by Yang Huizhi, are ingeniously designed, exquisitely detailed, and meticulously made, evoking reverence in those who behold them. On the walls, Wu Daozi painted the true forms of the Five Saints, as well as scenes from Laozi's temple and its rituals, which are so beautifully rendered that they surpass all other works in history. [Du Fu, in his poem, wrote: "At the pinnacle of the Yuan capital, the high enclosure is long and secluded. The strict rituals are maintained, and the sacred objects are guarded. The blue tiles shine beyond the early cold, and the jade pillars stand firm amidst the single breath of the sky. Mountains and rivers support the embroidered doors, and the sun and moon approach the carved beams. The immortal Li has deep roots, and the fragrant orchid shines brightly across generations. The old family has left behind its history, and the moral teachings are entrusted to the current king. The painter's hand looks back at the predecessors; Wu Daozi's skill far surpasses the field. The dense arrangement shifts the earth's axis, and the exquisite beauty stirs the palace walls. The Five Saints are arrayed in dragon robes, and a thousand officials line up like geese. The crowns and capes all shine brilliantly, and the flags and banners flutter freely. The cypress pines deeply retain their shadows, and the red pears stand out against the frost. The kites blow the jade pillars, and the well freezes the silver bed. The body retreats in humility before the Zhou dynasty, and the scriptures and traditions honor the Han emperor. The spirit of the valley, if it does not die,..."

To cultivate one's clumsiness, where else? At the Jing Ai Temple, there is a painting or sculpture of a "chicken-tail sick dragon," and no one knows who the painter is. The art is so ingenious and lifelike that it seems almost divine. The "chicken-tail" is located in the eastern wing of the Guanyin Hall in the Heavenly King section, where a red-clothed divine person holds a wild chicken, and the scene is so lifelike that it seems to be flying. In the Zheng Ping Ward, the An Guo Temple was built by Princess Yuzhen of the Ming Emperor's reign. The gatehouse is ninety feet high, and the top of the columns has no brackets. To the south of the hall is the Jing Si Institute, where jade was carved into the likeness of the Heavenly Master Laozi. Ye Fashan, Luo Gongyuan, and Zhang Guo painted them on the wall. To the south of the institute is a pond that draws water from the imperial canal. Stones are piled up to resemble the Three Mountains of Penglai, Fangzhang, and Yingzhou. Many of the nuns were former palace maids from the Shangyang Palace. To the east, it adjoins the National Academy. During the Xiantong period, a scholar said, "Every time there is a clear wind and bright moon, one can hear the sounds of the mountain and the pond, as if they are playing music." The Minister Lu wrote a poem saying, "The evening sun through the silk window stirs up the dust. The green pines around the hall do not know of spring. Look at the old men reciting scriptures, half of them were once palace entertainers."

Bai Juyi, the White Emissary, was stationed in Luoyang, where he had a high and unrestrained enthusiasm. Whenever there was a scenic place with clouds and springs, he would not leave it unexplored. He often traveled extensively and enjoyed the beauty of nature. Because of this, he wrote the biography of Mr. Drunken Recitation to narrate his experiences. Lu Shangshu Jian, a high-ranking official, had a villa near the Yi River. The pavilions and terraces were elegant and lofty. In winter, he would often go for a walk with his relatives, leaning on the railing and enjoying the view of Mount Song and Luoyang. Suddenly, light snowflakes began to fall, and his enthusiasm grew even higher. He started talking about his past inspection of Jinling, where he remembered the misty waters of southern China. He often saw the locals floating in small boats, eating gusi rice and perch. Recently, he thought about these memories as if they were still vivid in his mind. After a while, he suddenly saw two people wearing straw hats and following the riverbank. They were pulling a small boat made of reeds. On the front of the boat, there was a green canopy, and inside, a white-clad person and a monk were sitting together. At the back of the boat, there was a small stove with a bamboo steamer for cooking. A young servant was cooking fish and tea. The boat passed by the railing, and the sound of singing and laughing from the boat was very loud. Lu Shangshu Jian clapped his hands in amazement and did not know who they were. He sent someone to inquire, and they said, "This is Bai Juyi and the monk Faguang, who have come from Jianchun Gate to the Xiangshan Hermitage." After that, every time he met friends and relatives, they would talk about this incident, saying that it was a display of a lofty and unrestrained spirit.

The Encounter of Governor Yan with a Recluse in the Zhongnan Mountains At the end of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, Yan Shizé, the governor of Jianzhou, was originally a high-ranking official in charge of medical affairs during the reign of Emperor Muzong. He had a keen interest in Taoism and, on the Dragon Boat Festival, ventured into the Zhongnan Mountains to gather herbs. Losing his way among the steep cliffs, he wandered for several days without realizing it. His dried food supplies were exhausted, and there were no people living in the area for miles. Calculating the distance from the capital, he found himself more than five or six hundred miles away. Despite the deep and secluded forest, the scenery was beautiful. Suddenly, he saw a few thatched huts emerging beneath the pines and bamboos, surrounded by smoke and vines, with only a narrow path leading to them. Yan Shizé knocked on the door repeatedly for a long time, but no one came out. Peering through the gaps in the fence, he saw a man lying on a stone bed, reading a book. Opening the door, he approached the man, who then rose and straightened his clothes. After Yan Shizé bowed and explained his situation, he invited him to sit on a flat stone. He asked about recent events in the capital and inquired how many years had passed since the An-Shi Rebellion. The man replied that he had been living there since the time of the rebellion and had not left until now. Yan Shizé then recounted his journey and experiences in detail.

The food supply has been exhausted to the point of hunger. Please use food to save them. The one who can help said, "Living in the mountains with no smoke from cooking, there is something that can cure them. Thinking of your distant journey and what you have brought, it rises from the beams and rafters. Open the medicine bag and inside there are over a hundred beans like broad beans. Take a pot to the medicine room, gather firewood, and fetch water to cook. After a long time, it emits a fragrant smell. When looked at, it is already as big as a palm. It can be eaten now. When thirsty, take the remaining water from the pot to drink." The scholar felt half full and extremely satisfied. He said, "You must have a special destiny to reach here. From now on, for thirty years, you will not be hungry or thirsty. Your worldly desires and thoughts will become more peaceful. In the future, you will hold the position of a high official. You should take a place near Luofu Mountain. If you can leave behind worldly distractions, you will also attain the way to immortality. After being away from home for a long time, you can return." The scholar wished to return home because he was concerned about losing his way. "Do not worry; it is only two or three miles away. Meet a woodcutter, and you can follow him to the city gate." Upon leaving the mountain, there was indeed a woodcutter by the roadside. When asked about the woodcutter's name, there was no answer. After a few days, he reached the villages of Fan River. After returning to the capital, he did not enjoy trying other foods. He felt increasingly strong and clear-minded. He had the intention to ride a phoenix and a dragon. Wearing rough clothing and walking with a staff, he often stayed in rocky hills. The governor Lu, who enjoyed quiet contemplation, thought of meeting extraordinary people. A Daoist told him the story, and he invited the scholar to his residence. Upon hearing the story of the high official, he recommended the recluse for an official position. He became the governor of Jianxi from the position of deputy governor of Zizhou. At the age of ninety, he arrived at the prefecture and served for only one year. He resigned and returned to Luofu Mountain. When Wei, the chancellor, went to Guangnan, he sent someone to visit him. He was still in the mountains during the 14th year of Dazhong. When Xiang, the chancellor, passed through Jiang and Zhe, he summoned him to a banquet at the Gui Pavilion. He only drank a few cups of wine and did not eat anything else.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

In the past, the crows crying around General Deng Ai's flag was a sign of military victory, and the sound of crows in Zhang Rui's courtyard was a good omen for receiving an official position. From this, we can infer that not all omens are necessarily bad. On the day of one's birth or appointment, rain accompanied by hail is considered an ill omen. [Cui Hang and Doulu Zhuo] During the sixth year of the Ganfu era in the summer of May, the rebel Huang Chao was advancing from Guangling towards Xianghan. The court appointed Wang Du as the Southern Army Commander and Doulu and Cui as Chancellors on the same day. During the ceremony, the palace courtyard was filled with fog, and when they stood to greet at the Hall of Political Affairs, hail as large as chicken eggs fell. [It was the 23rd day of May.] Scholars believed this was an omen of misfortune for the state. The following year, the rebels attacked and captured the capital, and both Chancellors died in the conflict. Was this the will of heaven, or was it due to human actions? Li Xun saw a supernatural being leave wine for him. During the Xiantong era, there was a magistrate of Zhongmu named Li Xun who lived in a villa in Putian. He had a fierce and arrogant nature and did not fear ghosts or gods. Whenever he saw people drinking and pouring wine, he would become angry and stop them. One day, he suddenly fell ill with a severe cold and was lying under the eaves. Suddenly, a farmer stood before his bed and said, "Neighbors came to visit you and saw several people with thin and weak appearances, dressed in blue or purple. There was also a short servant carrying two pots of wine, and they climbed the steps together." Li Xun's wife and children saw nothing. The farmer said to Li Xun, "You always act arrogantly and are stingy with wine. Now we have several pots of fine wine and want to offer you a drink." Soon, they filled a large bowl with wine and forced him to drink until both pots were empty. The bed and bedding were soaked with the remaining wine. As they were about to leave, they said to Li Xun, "How did you act so stingy with wine before? From now on, your body will be weak and sick like someone suffering from a hangover." Li Xun remained in a state of illness for several months before recovering. Feng, the Magistrate of Zhengzhou, personally summoned Li Xun and spoke to him about the event.

On the Taoist Sages Emperor Wuzong was fond of immortality and magical arts. Many Daoist sages and magicians from all over the realm came to the capital. Zhao Guizhen, who investigated the profound mechanisms, was skilled in making lead and mercury, and had a clear and refreshing appearance. Those who saw him were all deeply respectful. He often said that in the process of flying refinement, live silver must be obtained. An imperial order was given to gather materials in LePing. Afterwards, a large-scale labor force was mobilized, and what was produced all contained stone and ore. Only through smelting could it be obtained. He performed ritual fasting and worship for several days, wrote imperial edicts, and placed them in caves and grottoes. Soon, an old man with a staff arrived and said, "Mountains and rivers hide treasures because of virtue and emerge. Especially an enlightened ruler who cultivates immortality as a thought, why do responses not arrive? The respected teacher no longer worries. The next day, he will follow the request." After speaking, he left, and no one knew where he went. That night, there was a sound like thunder, and the mountain ore opened up several zhang. Silver liquid suddenly erupted out, matching the amount used. Within the palace, the refinement was very much conducted, and outsiders rarely knew his method. There were also people from Jinling named Xu Yuanzhang and Wang Qiong, who were skilled in writing talismans and performing mysterious transformations, close to commanding spirits. In the early years of the Fanchang era, they were summoned to the capital, entering and exiting the palace. Emperor Wuzong said to them, "..."

I have heard that in the previous dynasty, there was a renowned figure named Ming Chongyan who was skilled in talismans and magical formulas. He once obtained tangerines from Mount Luofu to supply imperial fruits, traveling thousands of miles yet completing it within ten days. Though my virtue falls short of the previous dynasty, is your skill then inferior to that of Chongyan? Yuan Chang replied, "My training has not reached the profound mysteries. Traversing mountains and seas might be seen as a mockery of virtue." However, within a thousand miles, it can be done in a single day. The emperor said, "In the eastern capital, pomegranates are often presented, and they are already ripe. You should bring ten in this evening." Yuan Chang followed the command and departed. By dawn, the sleeping chamber was opened, and a golden dish containing pomegranates was placed on the imperial bed. Soon, a mid-level official presented the pomegranates, and the number of missing ones was reported to the emperor. Miraculous transformations and changes occur in this way. Wang Qiong is adept at transforming things, able to do anything. In winter, using medicine, several peach and apricot trees were cultivated. One night, all the flowers bloomed, fragrant and luxuriantly beautiful, and after a month, they began to wither. When the emperor grew weary and passed away, he returned to his true nature, and Wang Qiong was also exiled to the southern regions. Only Yuan Chang escaped, and no one knows where he is now. Zhou Zunshi of the Hao Tian Temple was ninety-seven years old during the Gan Fu period. He said that from childhood, he had lived on Dongting Mountain. His father and uncles, who were deeply hidden and had attained true Dao, were there. There was a man named Zhang Ru Hua, a prominent figure in Xiang and Han, who was absorbed in the mysteries of silence. One day, a sudden event occurred.

Gu carried wealth to seek the Dao in the rivers and lakes, and when he learned that  Yao had appeared in the world to cultivate and refine, he went directly to Dongting Lake to meet him. He exhausted everything he had to support incense and fire.  Yao knew his resolve and allowed him to sit at the top of the bank to watch over the alchemical furnace. Often, there were strange forms and bizarre appearances, deeply frightening people, and supernatural beings would come to gather. Despite the deep fear, Gu remained calm and composed, never showing any sign of disturbance. This continued for a whole year.  Yao then told him, "The elixir in the furnace is the Seven-Refined Spiritual Sand, not the Nine-Transformed Golden Pill, but if one eats it, it can restore the soul and return the spirit. I once asked several disciples to guard it, but none could withstand the fear and gave up. You have not been guarding it for long, yet you have endured so well. When the furnace was opened and the elixir was completed, only ten pills were produced. Each pill can add twelve years to your lifespan. Keep them in a chest, but I have not yet taught you the method of swallowing the elixir." Gu had been away from home for over a year and one day decided to return to visit his family.  Yao bid him farewell and said, "I know you cannot stay here long. From now on, you will be caught in the worldly net. If you still aspire to immortality, do not be attached to the mundane world. The elixir I gave you can add twelve years to your lifespan for each pill. If you are sick or dead, examine if it has not yet decayed. Even after ten days, it can make the dead come back to life. However, this matter concerns the underworld, so be cautious."

It cannot be easily given to those who are not sincerely devoted to the path. Anyone who wishes to use this medicine to save lives must burn incense and make a formal announcement, and I will help you. Ru Hua returned and was greatly respected by his village. His parents fell ill and passed away, and those who took the medicine all recovered. After a few years, he traveled to Dongting and anchored his boat on the bank of the river in Jinling. There was a good merchant named Xu Shize who traveled in more than ten large ships and also anchored in the same area. He had a son who was in his prime but died suddenly in the middle of the night without any illness. His parents, both old and frail, cried and refused to eat. Ru Hua felt pity for them and went to save them with the divine sand. At first, when he took it, it did not work, but after taking another dose, he suddenly revived. Wherever he went, the city gates were very strict, and officials were about to arrest him. Soon, two yellow-robed people appeared, holding red scrolls with writing. The Master Zhou of Dongting ordered the guard to be called, and a purple-robed person sat at the table. They stood up with their hairpins and ceremonial staffs and said to those around them, "The Immortal Master has come to summon us; we cannot stay here anymore." They then ordered him to be released and said to him, "Because of this, your life will be extended; you should strive to do good deeds." The Taoist divided half of the goods he had brought to repay Ru Hua. Ru Hua took fifty thousand coins and distributed them to the poor and needy. He arrived at Dongting and met with Yao.

"Ode of the Banished Immortal" [Composed by Dou Hongyu, Prefect of Taizhou] During the fifteenth year of the Tianbao era in the first month, An Lushan rebelled, captured Luoyang, and the imperial army suffered defeat, failing to hold the passes. The emperor fled to Shu, passing through Ma嵬驿, where the imperial army refused to advance. The emperor ordered the concubine, Yang Guifei, to commit suicide. Afterward, the imperial carriage continued its journey, passing through Luogu Pass. The emperor dismounted and climbed a high place, saying to the eunuch, "I hastily fled from Chang'an without bidding farewell to my ancestral temples. This mountain is extremely high, and I can see the Qin region. I now bid farewell to my ancestral temples from afar." He then dismounted, faced east, and bowed twice, sobbing and weeping. Those around him also wept. The emperor said to the eunuch, "If I had listened to Zhang Jiuling's advice, I would not have reached this point. I now order a messenger to go to Shaoyang to perform a grand sacrificial ceremony with three animals as offerings." [Zhang Jiuling, the Chancellor, often advised the emperor to execute An Lushan, but the emperor was angry and said, "Are you like Wang Yi, who misjudged Shi Le? If I had executed An Lushan, he would have killed me. Therefore, I dared not advise."] The emperor then remounted his horse and asked for a long flute, playing a piece. When the piece was completed, he wept bitterly and stood there for a long time. The officials quickly recorded the melody into a score. When the imperial carriage arrived in Chengdu, the score was presented to the emperor, who asked for its name but could not remember it.

Looking at those around him, he said, "What kind of tune is there?" while looking at Chang'an from Luogu. After getting off his horse, he searched for a long flute and played it in response to something above. After a long while, he said, "I understand now." Reflecting, he thought about "Jiu Ling" and had a different intention to name the tune "Zhexian Yuan" (Exile of the Immortal), relating it to the incident at Ma嵬. After the incident, separation due to chaos and disorder occurred. Someone from Xichuan obtained the tune, but there is no way to know how, and it is simply called "Jiannan Shenqu" (Divine Tune of the Southern Sword). Its melody is extremely mournful and surpasses all other tunes in its emotional depth. During the Dali period, people in Jiangnan were very fond of this tune. The Magistrate of Suzhou, Liu Changqing, was demoted to be the Magistrate of Muzhou and played the tune during a farewell banquet. Liu Changqing then composed the lyrics for the tune, feeling quite pleased with himself, and he may not have known the original story behind the tune. The lyrics are as follows: A clear river, the setting sun just beginning to dip low, Sadness fills the heart as a lone boat departs. Birds fly away over distant and nearby fields, People follow the flow of water east and west. White clouds stretch far and wide, The bright moon shines over both the front and back streams. Alone, I regret being exiled to Changsha, The lush spring grass by the river and pond. When I was young, I often heard older people talk about the story of the exiled immortal, And Liu Changqing's lyrics are very talented and beautiful, Related to the original story.

The Events and Feelings Vary; I Have Fully Grasped Them. Taking Advantage of My Leisure Time, I Hastily Composed Its Words and Ordered the Musicians to Sing It, So as to Widely Disseminate the Knowledge to Those Who Do Not Know. The Text Says: Barbarian dust invades the palace gates and rushes through the barriers. A golden carriage carries the beautiful face, but the moment of cloud and rain dissipates. When will the king return? Heartbroken, morning and evening, one looks back at thousands of mountains, gazing alone at the first moon at the edge of the sky, with beautiful eyebrows still curved. Regarding the "Zhexianyuan" (Exile's Lament) by Duan, the official of Sui Prefecture, he regards it as a sequence of the poem "Zhexianyuan." It is said that Liu Shizhou's poem is unknown in its true story, but in detail, it only thinks of the imperial concubine. It is said that Emperor Xuanzong ascended to Lao Valley, truly having thoughts of virtuous people. Duan's work was not described. Therefore, I expanded its words, intending to achieve both. Although my talent and emotion are shallow and clumsy, not matching the two gentlemen, the reasoning may be worth seeing. I give this to those who understand. The words say: Clear mountains obstruct the sun across the sky, with green layers before the king's horse. The golden carriage tours westward to Shu State, with the king's face looking east at the Qin Plain. At Qujiang, the soul is broken with fragrant grass, and the imperial concubine's sorrowful and fixed gaze is at the evening smoke. The long flute is blown out at this time. What words are only for the beautiful? **Hanyuan Hall** The Hanyuan Hall was built at the beginning of the state, excavating Dragon Head Hill as the foundation. The red terrace was built over fifty feet high, with two gates with perched phoenixes and flying cranes on both sides. The dragon-tail road emerges in front of the gate, and leaning on the railing, one looks down at the front mountain as if it were in the palms of hands. The hall is two li away from the five gates. Every New Year's Day, the imperial guards and the imperial entourage stay in the hall, with golden armor and ornate weapons mixed with brocade and embroidery, lined up with officials and generals, with foreign chieftains looking up at the jade throne, as if it were in the heavens. It is said that since the time of the Zhou and Han dynasties, there has never been such grandeur as the fall of the Sui dynasty. The capital city, since the chaos of Zhu Ci, has had no disturbances for nearly a hundred years, with harmonious relations between the emperor and his ministers, and peace and quietness in all directions, with the cultural prosperity of the Zhou and Han dynasties overshadowing the barbarians.

Throughout the empire, from the noblest scholars to the common people, everyone strives to be diligent, clean, and cautious in order to gain the approval of the times. Those who receive a salary remain in their positions, while farmers and merchants focus on their respective trades. The entire world has unified under a single system of writing and laws. After a long period of peace and prosperity, people gradually become more luxurious and indulgent. The nobility and wealthy families look down on farming, and military commanders and local governors may not be competent. There are complaints about the hardships of labor, and bandits appear in the counties and towns. However, the emperor is diligent, modest, and upright, with no extraordinary policies, and this allows enemies to invade the land. The imperial carriage is scattered and lost. During the periods of the Qin, Han, Wei, and Jin dynasties, when rulers were neglectful and officials were corrupt, how could they not collapse? Now, we see the list of virtuous people and initiate a period of revival. The hearts of millions of people have been renewed with the virtues of the Tang dynasty. The rites, music, punishment, and governance should not be neglected. The Qujiang Pool was originally an island in the Qin dynasty. During the Kaiyuan era, it was dredged and became a beautiful place. To the south there are the Ziyun Tower and the Furong Garden, and to the west there are the Xingyuan Garden and the Cien Temple. Flowers and plants surround it, and the scenery is beautiful. People from the capital enjoy it especially during the Zhonghe and Shangsi festivals. Green tents and curtains line the embankments, and colorful carriages and strong horses are lined up closely. During the Shangsi festival, the emperor grants a banquet to his ministers. The Jingzhao Prefecture holds grand banquets, and the counties of Chang'an and Wanyuan compete in their splendor. They display all kinds of fine brocades and precious objects. Hundreds of officials gather at the mountain pavilion, and they are granted performances by the Taichang and Jiaofang orchestras. There are several boats in the pool, and only the prime minister, three envoys, officials from the northern provinces, and scholars from the Hanlin Academy get to board them. Every year it stirs the capital and is seen as a grand spectacle. In summer, the reeds and grass turn green, and willow trees shade the area. The blue waves and red lotuses are beautiful and inviting. Those who appreciate beauty enjoy the fragrant days and clear scenes, and people ride together, carrying wine, and the activity never stops.

During the reign of Emperor Xuan, Pei Shao, the Inspector-Governor of Xuancheng, had just paid his respects in the morning and was about to leave the capital when the lotus flowers in Qujiang were in full bloom. He went for a stroll with several prominent scholars and officials from the imperial administration. They walked from the Great Wild Goose Pagoda Temple, dismissing their attendants and each leading a small servant, to the foot of the Ziyun Pavilion. There, they saw five or six people sitting by the water. Among them, a man in yellow clothing, half-drunk and very proud, was pointing and laughing with a carefree demeanor. Pei Shao felt slightly annoyed and bowed to ask them, "What official position do you hold, my esteemed fellow?" They replied hastily, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the county magistrate of Guangde in Xuancheng Prefecture." Pei Shao then asked, "What position do you hold, sir?" Pei Shao mimicked their response, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the governor of Xuancheng Prefecture." The man in yellow clothing and his companions fled in a panic, and the others also scattered in panic. The court officials clapped and laughed. Within a few days, the story spread throughout the capital. The officials at the personnel bureau investigated and found that there was a request from the county magistrate of Guangde to be transferred to Luojiang. Emperor Xuan, while still a prince, had heard about this and often joked about it with his brothers. When he ascended the throne, Pei Shao took up the reins of power. He then issued an imperial edict and said to the ministers, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the Vice-Chancellor and Chief Councillor."

The True Body In the fourteenth year of the Xiantong era, an edict was issued to escort the True Body from Fengxiang to the imperial city [The True Body is said to be the middle finger bone of the Historical Buddha, measuring one inch and eight fen in length, as clear and pure as jade, contained in a small golden coffin. A pagoda was formerly built in Fengxiang. The *Nirvana Sutra* of the Buddhist tradition states that the Buddha passed away in the Jeta Grove, and his body was stored in a golden coffin and silver casket, surrounded by sandalwood and burned. The gods divided the relics with eight diamond-like beings, leaving only four teeth, while the rest were burned to ashes. It is unclear where this bone came from]. The common people of the capital city rushed and gathered, from the Kaiyuan Gate to the Qichuan River, with carriages and horses coming day and night, and drinking and revelry filling the streets, which was called the unrestricted giving of alms [In the past, there was a society in the city dedicated to welcoming the True Body, with residents, young and old, contributing a coin every ten days. From the Kai Cheng period to the Xiantong period, calculating the accumulated funds, it was infinite, and thus widely spent. At that time, the prices of goods, especially tea and rice, were high, often reaching a hundred loads, and other goods were abundant].

On the day they arrived in the capital, the emperor and his princes personally ascended the city tower. In the streets and markets, silk and other materials were used to create shapes of dragons, phoenixes, elephants, and horses, while bamboo was fashioned into figures of monks, Buddhas, ghosts, and gods. Flags, flowers, canopies, and other decorations were arranged for over twenty miles, interspersed with singing, dancing, music, and the sounds of guards, monks chanting, and drums, filling the sky with noise. Among the common people, some laughed and leaped for joy, while others wept with sorrow. The sacred body was carried in a precious carriage and kept in the inner palace for several months. Soon after, Emperor Wenzong, tired of the imperial throne, secretly ordered it sent to Fengxiang. Before that, when the sacred body arrived in the city, at every cross street, people built small pagodas of bricks to worship, and various groups claimed to have had miraculous experiences. Some said it shook at night, others said it emitted light, all in order to seek donations and wealth, and many profited from this. When the emperor died, everything was immediately suppressed, and the pagodas in the streets were destroyed. Some curious individuals secretly asked about the source of the light, and it was said that looking through a piece of cloudstone from afar would make anyone believe it.

In the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Xizong (干符) of the Tang Dynasty, the Xing'an Temple in Chang'an once again had an Arhat who transmitted Buddhist teachings. The capital city was unified in following his teachings. His residence was adorned with golden and green decorations, and all the utensils were made of precious gems. He told others, "Burning incense and sitting in meditation, I often speak of the Western Paradise and the attainment of Nirvana." After his passing, he was posthumously honored as the Venerable Universal Illumination. Believers all shed tears. Liu the Commandant, Cui the Attendant, Yu the Attendant, and other high-ranking officials and court nobles were his disciples. On the day of his departure, everyone wore white mourning clothes. Those who followed him encouraged officials to observe fasting and receive his teachings, causing them to neglect state affairs. Pian often read the biographies of famous monks. During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, there was a monk named Gunavarman who lived in Jinling. At the monastery, Emperor Wen said to him, "I often wish to observe fasting and abstain from killing, but I am compelled by the demands of worldly life and cannot fully follow through. The teacher traveled thousands of miles to teach this country. How will you teach them?" The monk replied, "The Way lies in the heart, not in external actions. The law depends on oneself, not on others. The cultivation of emperors and commoners differs. For commoners, of low status and fame, their virtue does not reach far."

His teachings do not come from the inner chambers, and his words do not reach the servants and maids. If he does not restrain himself, endure hardship, practice goodness, and uphold precepts, how can he use his heart? Emperors regard the four seas as their home and the myriad people as their children. If he speaks good words, the scholars and commoners will all be pleased; if he spreads one good deed, the people and gods will be harmonized. Clear punishment does not shorten their lives, and light labor does not exhaust their strength. Discriminate between musical tones and set the seasons; if the musical tones are discriminated, then the wind and rain will be harmonized; if commands are timely, then the cold and heat will be regulated. Knowing the hunger of the people is how one achieves the absence of hunger; knowing the cold of the people is how one achieves the absence of cold. Thus, holding the fast is also great. Not killing is a great multitude. How can it be that by missing one meal and saving one bird's life, one achieves great benefit? Emperor Wen patted the table and sighed, praising the good and the kind for a long time before saying: Ordinary people are confused by distant principles, and Buddhist monks are stuck in near teachings. Those confused by distant principles say the highest path is empty talk; those stuck in near teachings are bound by verses. Such as this gentleman truly opens enlightenment and clarity, capable of speaking of the relationship between heaven and humanity.

During the Yuanhe period, the Jinshi Li He was skilled in composing songs and poems, and was highly esteemed and known by Han Wengong (Han Yu) among the scholarly and official class. He was frequently praised and recommended, and thus had great fame and reputation. At the time, Chancellor Yuan Zhen, who was older and had passed the Mingjing examination, also engaged in writing poetry and wished to form a friendship with Li He. One day, Yuan Zhen carried a gift to visit Li He's door. After looking at the visitor's card, Li He quickly ordered his servant to say, "What business does a person who passed the Mingjing examination have coming to see Li He?" The chancellor no longer cared about it, and left feeling ashamed and angry. Later, Yuan Zhen, who was selected for the imperial examination as a Left Shiyi and held important positions, became a mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites. He argued that Li He's ancestor's name, Jin, was taboo and thus he should not be allowed to take the Jinshi examination. This was also because Li He was considered light and frivolous and was criticized by his contemporaries, thus encountering setbacks and difficulties. Han Wengong regretted his talent and wrote a record of his argument to clarify the matter, but ultimately did not succeed. After the reigns of Emperors Wuzong and Xuanzong of Tang, each year, more than a thousand people took the Spring Officials' examination. Among them, some were known for their verses, continuing without interruption. How about Li Mei, Huangfu Song, Li Ruxi, Liang Wang, Mao Tao, Bei Xiu, Lai Hu, Jia Sui, Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan, Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue, Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui, whose works were well-known and appreciated. Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan were famous for their lyrics and compositions. Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue were famous for their regulated verse. Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui were famous for their ancient style. All of them worked hard on their literary skills but were hindered by failing to pass the examination. However, among them, several had beautiful and excellent works that spread throughout the country. Those who were merely fortunate to be included in the list cannot be compared to the truly talented ones.

钦定四库全书 子部十二 剧谈录 小说家【异闻之属】提要 【臣】等谨案剧谈録三卷唐康骈撰王定保摭言作唐骈葢传写之讹故也唐书艺文志作康軿以其字驾言证之二字义皆相合未详孰是然诸书引之皆作骈疑亦唐志误也骈池阳人干符四年登进士第官至崇文馆校书是书成于干寕二年皆记天寳以来事亦间以议论附之凡四十条今以太平广记勘之一一相合非当时全部収入即后人从广记抄合也此本末有临安府陈道人书籍舗刋行字葢犹影抄宋本如潘将军一条注中疑为潘鹘硉字今本剑侠传从广记剽掇此条讹为潘碎遂不可觧知此本为善矣其中载元微之年老擢第执贽谒 李贺一条古夫于亭杂録辨之曰案元擢第既非迟暮于贺亦称前辈讵容执防造门反轻薄小説之不根如此其论最当然稗官所述半出传闻真伪互陈其风自古未可全以为据亦未可全以为诬在读者考证其得失耳不以是废此一家也乾隆四十一年十月恭校上
